The sentence is like the conclusion extracted from the second parable: وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ اجْتُثَّتْ مِنْ فَوْقِ الْأَرْضِ ما لَها مِنْ قَرارٍ (And the parable of a bad word is that of a bad tree: uprooted from the ground, it has no stability). The meaning is that Allah leads the people of disbelief astray by depriving them of the path of guidance; thus, they are not guided to a happy life in the worldly life, nor to an enduring blessing and pleasure from Allah in the hereafter. Nothing is found with them—if their hearts were uncovered—except doubt, hesitation, anxiety, agitation, sorrow, regret, and grief.

His saying: وَيَفْعَلُ اللهُ ما يَشاءُ — "and Allah does whatever He wishes" (14:27). That is, the fortification of these and the misguiding of those proceeds according to what His will necessitates; there is no preventer for Him nor any repeller. There is no barrier between His will and His act.

From this, it appears that Allah, the Exalted, has willed the fortification of these and the misguiding of those, and He is their Doer inevitably. Thus, the felicity of the believer and the wretchedness of the disbeliever are among the "decreed judgments" (القضاء المحتوم), as has been mentioned in the narrations.

The occurrence of the Name of Majesty (لفظ الجلالة) in His saying وَيُضِلُّ اللهُ (and Allah leads astray) and His saying وَيَفْعَلُ اللهُ (and Allah does) is the use of the manifest noun in place of the pronoun, and it indicates the magnitude of the matter and the awe of the station, as has been said.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دارَ الْبَوارِ — "Have you not regarded those who substituted ungratefulness for Allah’s blessing, and settled their people in the abode of ruin?" (14:28). It is said in al-Majma': "Settling" (الإحلال) is placing a thing in a location (محل), either by proximity if it is of the category of bodies, or by inherence if it is of the category of accidents. "Ruin" (البوار) is destruction (الهلاك). It is said: "the thing was ruined" (بار الشيء يبور بورا) when it perished. A man is "ruined" (بور) meaning perished, and a people are "ruined" (بور) as well. End quote.

Al-Raghib said: "Ruin" (البوار) is excessive stagnation (فرط الكساد). Since excessive stagnation leads to corruption—as it is said: "it stagnated until it corrupted"—ruin was used to express destruction. It is said: "the thing was ruined" (بار الشيء يبور بورا وبؤرا). Allah, the All-Mighty and Majestic, said: تِجارَةً لَنْ تَبُورَ — "a commerce that will never perish" (35:29). End quote.

The verse mentions the state of the imams of disbelief (أئمة الكفر) and the leaders of misguidance (رؤساء الضلال) in their wrongdoing and their ingratitude toward the blessing of Allah, the Glorified, which surrounded them from every side, instead of thanking Him and believing in their Lord. He had previously mentioned how He, the Exalted, created the heavens and the earth out of His Self-Sufficiency—which is a blessing—then He mentioned the word of truth to which He calls and its steady, good effects—which is a blessing.

The verse is absolute; there is no evidence for restricting it to the disbelievers of Mecca or the disbelievers of Quraysh, even if the address in it is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The presence of phrases at its end like قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى النَّارِ (Say, ‘Enjoy! Indeed your destination is the Fire’) does not necessitate a restriction in the verse, given the absolute nature of its content and its inclusion of the tyrants (الطواغيت) of the nations and what they did to their peoples.

Thus, His saying أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً (Have you not regarded those who substituted ungratefulness for Allah’s blessing) mentions the state of the imams of disbelief and leaders of misguidance from the past nations and from this nation. The evidence for its restriction to them is His saying وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دارَ الْبَوارِ (and settled their people in the abode of ruin), which suggests their word was influential and they were obeyed among their people; thus, they are the imams and leaders.

What is meant by their "substituting ungratefulness for Allah’s blessing" is substituting disbelief (كفراً) for the gratitude (شكر) for His blessing that was obligatory upon them. Thus, in the sentence, there is a deleted "annexed noun" (مضاف محذوف), the estimation being: "they substituted disbelief for the gratitude for Allah's blessing." It is also possible that what is meant is the substitution of the blessing itself with disbelief by a type of metaphor (تجوز). Similar to the verse in this regard is the saying of the Exalted: وَتَجْعَلُونَ رِزْقَكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ تُكَذِّبُونَ — "and make your denial your [only] share [of it]?" (56:82).

The mention of their settling their people in the abode of ruin necessitates their settling themselves in it as well, because they are imams of misguidance who went astray and then led others astray, and the consequence is the consequence of misguidance. Similar to the verse in this meaning is His saying regarding Pharaoh: يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ — "He will lead his people on the Day of Resurrection and conduct them into the Fire" (11:98).

The meaning is: Have you not looked at the imams and leaders from the past nations and from your nation who substituted disbelief for the gratitude for Allah's blessing, and their people followed them, so they settled and settled their people in the abode of destruction—which is wretchedness and the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَها وَبِئْسَ الْقَرارُ — "Hell, which they shall enter—and it is an evil abiding place" (14:29). This is an exposition (بيان) of the "abode of ruin." The possibility raised by some—that "Hell" (جَهَنَّمَ) is in the accusative due to "distraction" (الاشتغال), the estimation being "they shall enter Hell, they shall enter it," and the sentence being a new start (مستأنفة)—is devoid of merit because the accusative is less preferred and there is no subtle point necessitating a new start.

From here, the invalidity of the view of those who said the verses are Medinan and that what is meant by "those who disbelieved" are the grandees of Mecca and the chiefs of Quraysh who gathered the masses against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and fought him at Badr, so they were killed and settled their people in the abode of ruin, becomes clear.

This is because you have learned from the meaning of the verse that it is absolute, and there is no reason to restrict it to the slain of Badr among the disbelievers at all. Rather, the verse includes every imam of misguidance who settled his people in the abode of ruin, from those who preceded and those who followed. What is meant by "settling in the abode of ruin" is establishing them in the wretchedness of the Fire, even if they have not yet been killed, died, or entered the Fire.

Furthermore, the apparent meaning of the following verse—وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ أَنْداداً لِيُضِلُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى النَّارِ (They set up equals to Allah to lead [people] astray from His way. Say, ‘Enjoy! Indeed your destination is the Fire’)—is that the plural pronoun returns to "those who disbelieved" mentioned in this verse. This would necessitate that the address "Say, ‘Enjoy!’" be an address to those remaining among them—namely, those who embraced Islam on the day of the Conquest—which would be a threat of certain, realized wretchedness without exception.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ أَنْداداً لِيُضِلُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى النَّارِ — "They set up equals to Allah to lead [people] astray from His way. Say, ‘Enjoy! Indeed your destination is the Fire’" (14:30). "Equals" (الأنداد) is the plural of "equal" (ند), which is a peer (المثل). They are the deities they took as gods besides Allah from among the angels, jinn, and humans.

They only made them "equals" while confessing that they were created by Allah, the Glorified, from the perspective that they named them gods and lords, attributed the management of the world's affairs to them, and then worshipped them out of fear and hope. This was despite the fact that the command and the creation belong entirely to Allah—as their primordial nature confessed and as Allah supported with the signs and proofs He inspired in His prophets and messengers indicating His oneness.

Thus, they were acting with insight regarding the matter of monotheism (التوحيد); they did not take equals out of heedlessness or error. Rather, they did so seeking the fleeting goods of the worldly life and to enslave people and exploit them by leading them astray from the way of Allah. Therefore, He explained the reason for their taking equals with His saying: لِيُضِلُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ (to lead [people] astray from His way). Then He commanded the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to threaten them with the Fire, to which is their return and they have no return other than it, saying: قُلْ تَمَتَّعُوا فَإِنَّ مَصِيرَكُمْ إِلَى النَّارِ (Say, ‘Enjoy! Indeed your destination is the Fire’).

The natural course of the speech would have been to say to them: "Take equals" or "Lead astray from the way of Allah, for your destination is the Fire." However, it was substituted with His saying تَمَتَّعُوا (Enjoy!) to explicitly state their corrupt purpose which they were hiding, so as to be more eloquent in their exposure.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لِعِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَيُنْفِقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ سِرًّا وَعَلانِيَةً مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ يَوْمٌ لا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَلا خِلالٌ — "Say to My servants who have faith that they should maintain the prayer and spend out of what We have provided them, secretly and openly, before there comes a day in which there will be no bargaining, nor friendship" (14:31). When He threatened them through the tongue of His Messenger with the punishment of the Day of Resurrection for leading people astray from His way, He commanded him to order His servants who believed to adhere to His way before the Day of Resurrection comes, when they will not be able to rectify what they missed of felicity through any of the means circulating among them for that purpose. These means return to one of two things: either exchange by giving something and taking its equivalent—which is "bargaining" (البيع) in its more general sense—or "friendship" (الخلة) and love. None of these means have any effect on a Day purely for reckoning and recompense; for that is the nature of the Day of Resurrection and nothing else.

From here, it appears that His saying يُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَيُنْفِقُوا (they should maintain the prayer and spend) is an exposition of the "way of Allah" (سبيل الله). He sufficed with these two pillars by which all other religious duties are joined—those that rectify man's worldly life in what is between him and his Lord, and what is between him and the other members of his species.

His saying يُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَيُنْفِقُوا (they should maintain the prayer and spend), etc., are in the jussive case (مجزومان) because they occur as the response to the command (جواب الأمر). The "content of the saying" (مقول القول) is deleted because the two verbs indicate it. The estimation is: "Say: 'Maintain the prayer and spend (etc.)'; they will maintain the prayer and spend (etc.)."

The objection—that the verb in the jussive as a response to a command must be a necessary consequence of it, and it does not follow from the command to pray and spend that they will obey it—[is addressed elsewhere].

...is invalid, for it is necessary that the response be something required by the command in some respect; and His commanding His believing servants—who are indeed believing servants—is something that necessitates obedience without any doubt.

The spending (الإنفاق) mentioned in the verse refers to spending in the way of Allah in a general sense, as the Surah is Meccan and the verse of zakat (الزكاة) — the obligatory alms-tax — had not yet been revealed. The meaning of spending "secretly and openly" is that the spending should proceed according to what is necessitated by true religious etiquette (الأدب الديني); thus, one keeps it secret where secrecy is appropriate and makes it public where publicity is appropriate. What is sought by this, in any case, is to perform that which rectifies what is liable to corruption and straightens the affairs of the Muslim community.

The negation of "friendship" (المخالة) in this verse does not contradict His saying, the Exalted: الْأَخِلَّاءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ إِلَّا الْمُتَّقِينَ "On that day friends will be one another’s enemies, except for the God-conscious" (43:67). This is because the relationship between the two verses is one of absolute generality and specificity (العموم والخصوص المطلق); thus, this verse is specified by that one. The meaning derived from both verses is that every friendship (خلة) not based on godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and boundaries — will be severed on the Day of Resurrection. As for the friendship based on it, which is friendship for the sake of Allah's essence, it remains firm and beneficial. Thus, the absolute negation of friendship followed by the affirmation of some of it in the two verses is similar to the absolute negation of intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — in His saying: وَلا خُلَّةٌ وَلا شَفاعَةٌ "...neither shall there be any friendship or intercession" (2:254), followed by its affirmation for those who have Allah's permission, as in His saying: إِلَّا مَنْ شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ "...excepting such as witness to the truth and have knowledge" (43:86).

As for what has been said regarding the negation of contradiction—that the "friendship" (الخلال) in the negating verse refers to friendship based on worldly means to compensate for what was missed, unlike the affirming verse; or that the negated friendship is that which follows natural inclination and the soul's desire, unlike the affirmed friendship which is for the sake of Allah's essence—both views essentially return to what we have mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ "It is Allah who created the heavens and the earth..." (14:32) etc. When the Glorified mentioned that they set up equals (أندادا) to Allah to mislead people from the way of Allah and threatened them for it, He presented in this verse, through to the end of the next three verses, the proof for the exclusivity of Lordship (الربوبية) to Himself, the Exalted and Sanctified. This is established through the exclusivity of general governance (التدبير العام) to Him, manifested in the order of creation, the sending down of water, the bringing forth of sustenance, and the subjugation (تسخير) of the seas—the ships (الفلك)—the rivers, the sun, the moon, the night, and the day.

He pointed out at the end of the verses that these, and countless others, are blessings (نعمة) from Him, the Exalted, to man. This is because the discourse in this Surah—as previously indicated—proceeds in the light of the two Names: the All-Mighty (العزيز), the All-Laudable (الحميد).

Thus, His saying: اللهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ "It is Allah who created..." is in the sense of our saying: "He alone is the Lord, to the exclusion of those you have set up as equals to Him."

And His saying: وَأَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَخْرَجَ بِهِ "...and sent down water from the sky, and with it brought forth..." (14:32). What is meant by "the sky" (السماء) is the direction of height, which is its linguistic meaning. The water descending from it is the rain, for to it returns all the water on earth by which living things—plants and animals—survive.

His saying, the Exalted: وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ لِتَجْرِيَ فِي الْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْأَنْهارَ "...and He disposed the ships for you to sail on the sea by His command, and He disposed the rivers for you" (14:32). The subjugation (تسخير) of the ships for people means making them such that they benefit them in their objectives, such as transporting themselves and their cargo, without sinking in the water or being unable to move.

As for the view of some that their "subjugation" means giving people the power to manufacture and use them by inspiring them with the method—this is far-fetched. For the apparent meaning of subjugating something for man is to act upon that thing by making it conform to the benefits he seeks, not to act upon man himself through inspiration and the like.

The natural flow of the speech would have been to say: "He disposed the sea for you so that the ships may sail in it by His command, and He disposed the rivers for you." However, it was reversed, and it was said: وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ لِتَجْرِيَ فِي الْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِ "...and He disposed the ships for you to sail on the sea by His command," because the ship is among the clearest of maritime blessings, even if His blessings are not limited to it. Perhaps this is the reason for the reversal, as the context is one of enumerating blessings, and the blessing in the ship is clearer, even if the blessing in the sea is greater.

The attribution of their sailing in the sea to His command (أمره), the Exalted, despite it being attributed to active natural causes (الأسباب الطبيعية) such as wind, steam, and other factors, is because He, the Exalted, is the encompassing Cause (السبب المحيط) to whom every cause ultimately returns.

And His saying: وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْأَنْهارَ "...and He disposed the rivers for you," which are the flowing waters in various regions of the earth. Their subjugation means making them subservient (تدليلها) such that man benefits from them for drinking, washing, removing filth, and other uses, and by which the animals and plants subjugated to him survive.

His saying, the Exalted: وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ دائِبَيْنِ وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ "And He disposed the sun and the moon for you, constant [in their courses], and He disposed the night and the day for you" (14:33). Al-Raghib said: "The word da'b (الدأب) means the continuation of travel... He, the Exalted, said: دائِبَيْنِ 'constant [in their courses]'. Al-da'b also means a habit that continues constantly in one state, as He, the Exalted, said: كَدَأْبِ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ 'Like the custom of Pharaoh’s clan' (8:52), meaning like their habit which they continued upon." [End of quote]. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآتاكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ "And He gave you all that you asked Him. If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them. Indeed man is most unjust and ungrateful" (14:34). Asking (السؤال) is requesting (الطلب), but it differs in that asking is only from one who possesses intellect, while requesting is more general. Man only becomes aware of asking due to the need that calls for it; thus, it becomes apparent to him to seek the removal of the need that has befallen him. The usual means for this is speech, so he employs it; sometimes he employs a gesture or writing, and it is truly called "asking" without metaphor.

Since Allah, the Glorified, is the One who removes the need of every needy being besides Him, and nothing in its existence or survival attaches itself to anything except the hem of His bounty and generosity—whether it acknowledges Him or denies Him—and He, the Exalted, knows them and their outward and inward needs better than they know themselves, every being besides Him is a devotee at the door of His bounty, asking Him to remove the need that has befallen it, whether He gives or withholds, and whether He answers all that was asked or some of it.

This is the true nature and reality of asking (السؤال), which is exclusive to Him, the Exalted, and does not extend to anyone else. Some asking is verbal—as mentioned—by which Allah, the Glorified, may be asked, or others may be asked. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the One asked; everything asks Him through the reality of asking, and some people among the believers ask Him through verbal asking.

This is regarding the asking; as for the giving (الإيتاء), it has been mentioned generally without being restricted by an exception or the like, which indicates that there is no asking except that there is a giving with it. This is a contextual clue that the address is to the species (النوع), as is also supported by the conclusion: إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ "Indeed man is most unjust and ungrateful."

The meaning is that the human species, in its specific nature, has not needed any blessing except that Allah has fulfilled its need, either entirely or partially, even if an individual (الفرد) might need and ask but his need is not granted.

This meaning is supported by His saying, the Exalted: أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ "I answer the solicitor’s invocation when he delivers it to Me" (2:186). It was mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of that verse that He, the Exalted, does not reject the supplication of one who calls upon Him, unless it is not a true supplication, or it is a supplication but not to Him, but rather to someone else. An individual human might have his tongue disagree with his heart or speak nonsense in his supplication, but the species, in its specific nature, knows neither vanity nor hypocrisy, and knows no Lord other than Him, the Glorified. Whenever a need touches it, it asks Him in reality, and asks none but its Lord; thus, all its supplications are answered, its requests are granted, and its needs are fulfilled.

It has become clear from what preceded that the word "from" (مِنْ) in His saying: مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ "of all that you asked Him" is for beginning (ابتدائية), indicating that what Allah gives is taken from what they asked, whether it was all they asked, as in some cases, or some of it, as in others. If it were partitive (تبعيضية), it would imply that He gives in every asking only a part of what was asked, and the reality is otherwise. Similarly, if it were said: "And He gave you all that you asked Him," it would imply giving everything, which is not the case. If it were said: "of what you asked Him," it would imply that it is possible for some supplications not to be answered and some requests to be rejected from the root, and the verse—being in the context of reminding of favors—rejects that.

In summary, the meaning of the verse is that Allah the Exalted gave the human species what it asked; there is no need among its needs except that He removed all or some of it according to what His profound wisdom necessitates.

It has been said that the implied meaning is: "And He gave you of all that you asked Him and what you did not ask Him," but this is based on the assumption that "asking" refers only to verbal asking, and the opposite has already been established, and the context of the verse does not support it.

And His saying: وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها "If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them" (14:34). Al-Raghib said: "Al-ihsa' (الإحصاء) is obtaining by number... it is derived from the word al-hasa (الحصى) — pebbles — and its use in this sense is because they used to rely on them for counting as we rely on fingers." [End of quote].

The sentence contains an indication that the blessings exceed the power of enumeration (الإحصاء), and its necessary consequence is that the human needs which Allah has removed through His blessings are beyond man's ability to count.

And how can His blessings, the Exalted, be counted when the world of existence (عالم الوجود) with all its parts and the attributes and states attached to them are interconnected and organized, some benefiting others and some depending on others? Thus, everything is a blessing (نعمة) in relation to everything else, and this is a matter that no enumeration can encompass.

Perhaps that is the secret behind the singular form of "blessing" in His saying: نِعْمَتَ اللهِ "Allah's blessing," for the truth is that there is nothing but the blessing; thus, there is no need to emphasize it with the plural to indicate multiplicity. What is meant by "blessing" is the genus of what is bestowed (جنس المنعم), thus conveying what the plural conveys.

And His saying: إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ "Indeed man is most unjust and ungrateful," meaning very ungrateful (كثير الكفران), oppressing himself by not thanking Allah for the blessing and being ungrateful for it, which leads him to ruin and loss; or very unjust (كثير الظلم) toward Allah's blessings by not thanking them and being ungrateful for them. The sentence is an explanatory resumption (استئناف بياني) by which what was understood from the previous explanation is emphasized. For one who stands upon what has been explained regarding the state of His blessings, the Exalted, and what He has given man of all that he asked of them, will not doubt that man—being naturally heedless of them—is unjust to himself and ungrateful for the blessing of his Lord.

(Commentary on Narrations)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated by al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, al-Bazzar, Abu Ya'la, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Hibban, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and Ibn Mardawayh, from Anas, who said: "A tray of fresh dates was brought to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and he said: مَثَلاً كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ '...a parable: a good word is like a good tree...' until he reached تُؤْتِي أُكُلَها كُلَّ حِينٍ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّها 'It gives its produce every season by the leave of its Lord' (14:24-25). He said: 'It is the date palm.' وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ 'And the parable of a bad word is a bad tree...' until he reached ما لَها مِنْ قَرارٍ '...having no stability' (14:26). He said: 'It is the colocynth (الحنظلة).'"

I say: That the "good tree" is the date palm is narrated in several reports from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and they do not indicate more than the application (انطباق) of the parable to it. The end of the narration contradicts the following one.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "A group of the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sat and mentioned this verse: اجْتُثَّتْ مِنْ فَوْقِ الْأَرْضِ ما لَها مِنْ قَرارٍ '...uprooted from the ground, having no stability' (14:26). They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, we see it as the truffle (الكمأة).' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'The truffle is from the manna (المن), and its water is a cure for the eye; and the ajwah date is from Paradise, and it is a cure for poison.'"

I say: The same applies to the colocynth, for it has important medicinal properties.

In the same source, al-Bayhaqi narrated in his Sunan from Ali (عليه‌السلام) who said: "The 'season' (الحين) is six months."

I say: The discussion regarding this is like the discussion regarding the previous one.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission to 'Amr ibn Hurayth, who said: "I asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُها ثابِتٌ وَفَرْعُها فِي السَّماءِ '...like a good tree: its roots are steady and its branches are in the sky' (14:24). He said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is its root, and the Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام) is its branch, and the Imams from their progeny are its boughs, and the knowledge of the Imams is its fruit, and their believing followers (الشيعة) are its leaves. Is there any excess in this?' I said: 'No, by Allah.' He said: 'By Allah, indeed when a believer is born, a leaf sprouts on it, and when a believer dies, a leaf falls from it.'"

I say: The narration is based on the "good word" (الكلمة الطيبة) being the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The term "word" (الكلمة) has been applied in His speech to a human, as in His saying: بِكَلِمَةٍ مِنْهُ اسْمُهُ الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ "...with a word from Him whose name is Messiah, Jesus son of Mary" (3:45). Nevertheless, the narration is by way of application (التطبيق). Evidence for this is the variation in narrations regarding the manner of application: in some, the root is the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the branch is Ali (عليه‌السلام), the boughs are the Imams (عليه‌السلام), the fruit is their knowledge, and the leaves are the followers (الشيعة), as in this narration. In others, the tree is the Messenger of Allah, its branch is Ali, the bough is Fatimah, its fruit is her children, and its leaves are our followers, as narrated by al-Saduq from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him). In yet others, the Prophet and the Imams are the steady root, and the branch is the mastership (الولاية) for whoever enters into it, as in al-Kafi from Muhammad al-Halabi from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him).

In al-Majma', Abu al-Jarud narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): "This—meaning His saying: كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ '...like a bad tree...' etc.—is a parable for the Umayyads (بني أمية)."

In the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, from 'Abd al-Rahman ibn Salim al-Ashal from his father from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ "Allah draws a parable: a good word is like a good tree..." the two verses. He said: "This is a parable Allah drew for the People of the House (أهل بيت) of His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and for those who show enmity toward them, it is the parable of كَلِمَة خَبِيثَة كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ ـ اجْتُثَّتْ مِنْ فَوْقِ الْأَرْضِ ما لَها مِنْ قَرارٍ '...a bad word is a bad tree: uprooted from the ground, having no stability.'"

I say: Al-Alusi said in the Tafsir of Ruh al-Ma'ani, verbatim: "The Imamiyyah—and you know their state—narrated from Abu Ja'far (may Allah be pleased with him) the interpretation of it—meaning the bad tree—as the Umayyads, and the interpretation of the good tree as the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), Ali (may Allah ennoble his face), Fatimah (may Allah be pleased with her), and what was born of them. In some narrations of the Sunnis, there is that which troubles the interpretation of the bad tree as the Umayyads. Ibn Mardawayh narrated from 'Adi ibn Hatim who said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: Indeed Allah the Exalted turned the servants over, inside and out, and the best of His servants were the Arabs; and He turned the Arabs over, inside and out, and the best of the Arabs were Quraysh; and it is the blessed tree of which Allah the Exalted said in His Book: مَثَلاً كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ "a parable: a good word is like a good tree," because the Umayyads are from Quraysh.'" [End of the relevant portion].

This is strange, for the fact that a nation or a group is blessed according to their nature does not necessitate that all the branches branching from it are likewise. The narration, assuming its acceptance, only indicates that Quraysh is a blessed tree. As for all the branches branching from it being blessed and good—such as the Banu 'Abd al-Dar, for example—or every individual among them being so—such as Abu Jahl and Abu Lahab—certainly not. What necessary connection is there between a tree being blessed and good in its root, and some of its branches that separated from it and grew in a corrupt growth being blessed and good?

Ibn Mardawayh narrated this from 'Aishah: that she said to Marwan ibn al-Hakam: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying to your father and your grandfather: 'Indeed you are the cursed tree (الشجرة الملعونة) in the Quran.'"

The authors of the Tafsir works, such as al-Tabari and others, narrated from Sahl ibn Sa'd, 'Abdullah ibn 'Umar, Ya'la ibn Murrah, al-Husayn ibn Ali, and Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib: that they are the ones regarding whom His saying, the Exalted, was revealed: وَما جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْناكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ ـ وَالشَّجَرَةَ الْمَلْعُونَةَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ "...We did not make the vision We showed you except as a tribulation for the people and the tree cursed in the Quran..." (17:60). The wording of Sa'd is: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saw the children of so-and-so leaping upon his pulpit like the leaping of monkeys, and that distressed him, so he did not laugh fully until he died, and Allah revealed: وَما جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا '...We did not make the vision...'"

The narration from 'Umar and from Ali will follow in the commentary of His saying: الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً "...those who changed Allah’s blessing for ungratefulness..." (14:28), that they are the two most wicked groups of Quraysh: the Banu al-Mughirah and the Banu Umayyah.

In the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, from Safwan ibn Mihran from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Satan comes to a man from our friends (أوليائنا) at the time of his death, coming to him from his right and his left to turn him away from what he is upon, but Allah refuses that. Thus Allah said: يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ 'Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter' (14:27)."

In the same source, from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon them), who said: "When a man is placed in his grave, two angels come to him—an angel from his right and an angel from his left—and Satan is stood before him, his eyes like copper. It is said to him: 'What do you say about this man who came out from among you, claiming to be the Messenger of Allah?' He becomes terrified by that. If he is a believer, he says: 'Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah.' It is said at that: 'Sleep a sleep in which there is no dream,' and his grave is expanded for him nine cubits, and he sees his seat in Paradise. This is the saying of Allah: يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا 'Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world.' If he is a disbeliever, they say: 'Who is this man who was among you, saying that he is the Messenger of Allah?' He says: 'I do not know.' Then he is left between him and Satan."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated by al-Tayalisi, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh, from al-Bara' ibn 'Azib, that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "When the Muslim is questioned in the grave, he bears witness that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah. That is His saying, the Glorified: يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ ـ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ 'Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter' (14:27)."

In the same source, al-Tabarani in al-Awsat and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying regarding this verse: يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ ـ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ 'Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter'—he said: 'In the Hereafter is the grave.'"

I say: There are many narrations from the paths of the Shi'a and the Sunnis regarding the details of the questioning in the grave, the coming of the two angels Munkar and Nakir, and the firmness of the believer and the straying of the disbeliever at that time, and many of them have relied upon this verse.

Their apparent meaning is that what is meant by "the Hereafter" (الآخرة) is the grave and the world of death. Perhaps this is based on the apparent meaning of "fortifying" (التثبيت), for the apparent meaning of giving firmness is that it be in a place where slipping and confusion are possible. This is only conceivable in other than the Day of Resurrection, in which there is nothing but the recompense for deeds. However, considering that everything firm in existence is only firm through Allah, the Glorified—whether it is something liable to cessation or not—there is no difference between the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — and the Resurrection in that the believer is firm through the fortification of Allah, the Glorified. It is better to take the narrations as being of the nature of application (التطبيق).

In the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah, who said: "The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said regarding His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً 'Have you not regarded those who changed Allah’s blessing for ungratefulness...' (14:28): 'We are the blessing of Allah which Allah bestowed upon the servants.'"

I say: This is from the category of flow and application (الجري والتطبيق).

In the same source, from Mi'sam al-Musrif from Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دارَ الْبَوارِ "...and landed their people in the abode of ruin?" (14:28). He said: "They are the two most wicked groups of Quraysh: the Banu Umayyah and the Banu al-Mughirah." I say: It was also narrated in al-Burhan from Ibn Shahr Ashub from Abu al-Tufayl from him (عليه‌السلام).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani in al-Awsat, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated through various paths from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً "Have you not regarded those who changed Allah’s blessing for ungratefulness..." (14:28). He said: "They are the two most wicked groups of Quraysh: the Banu Umayyah and the Banu al-Mughirah. As for the Banu al-Mughirah, Allah cut off their roots on the day of Badr; as for the Banu Umayyah, they were given enjoyment for a time."

I say: This is narrated from 'Umar as will follow.

In the same source, al-Bukhari in his Tarikh, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from 'Umar ibn al-Khattab regarding His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً "Have you not regarded those who changed Allah’s blessing for ungratefulness..." (14:28). He said: "They are the two most wicked groups of Quraysh: the Banu al-Mughirah and the Banu Umayyah. As for the Banu al-Mughirah, you were spared from them on the day of Badr; as for the Banu Umayyah, they were given enjoyment for a time."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn 'Abbas: that he said to 'Umar: "O Commander of the Faithful, this verse: الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً '...those who changed Allah’s blessing for ungratefulness...'?" He said: "They are the two most wicked groups of Quraysh: my maternal uncles and your paternal uncles. As for my maternal uncles, Allah eradicated them on the day of Badr; as for your paternal uncles, Allah gave them respite for a time."

In the Tafsir of al-'Ayyashi, from Dharih from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I heard him saying: Ibn al-Kawwa' came to the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) and asked him about the saying of Allah: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا 'Have you not regarded those who changed...' the verse. He said: 'That is Quraysh; they changed Allah's blessing for ungratefulness and denied His Prophet on the day of Badr.'"

I say: The variation in application (التطبيق) in his (عليه‌السلام) speech is evidence that it is from the category of explaining the verse's applicability, not from the category of the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول).

In al-Kafi, from Ali ibn Muhammad from some of his companions in a marfu' (مرفوع) — attributed to the Imam — narration, he said: "When Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) read this verse: وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها 'If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them' (14:34), he would say: 'Glory be to Him who did not place in anyone any knowledge of His blessings except the knowledge of falling short in knowing them, just as He did not place in anyone any knowledge of perceiving Him more than the knowledge that he cannot perceive Him. Thus, He, the Majestic and Exalted, thanked the knowledge of the knowers regarding their falling short in knowing how to thank Him, and He made their knowledge of falling short a form of thanks; just as He knew the knowledge of the knowers that they cannot perceive Him, and He made that knowledge.'" [The narration continues].

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ رَبِّ ٱجْعَلْ هَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدَ ءَامِنًا وَٱجْنُبْنِى وَبَنِىَّ أَن نَّعْبُدَ ٱلْأَصْنَامَ (٣٥)
When Abraham said, ‘My Lord! Make this city a sanctuary, and save me and my children from worshiping idols. (35)
رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۖ فَمَن تَبِعَنِى فَإِنَّهُۥ مِنِّى ۖ وَمَنْ عَصَانِى فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣٦)
My Lord! Indeed they have misled many people. So whoever follows me indeed belongs to me, and as for those who disobey me, well, You are indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (36)
رَّبَّنَآ إِنِّىٓ أَسْكَنتُ مِن ذُرِّيَّتِى بِوَادٍ غَيْرِ ذِى زَرْعٍ عِندَ بَيْتِكَ ٱلْمُحَرَّمِ رَبَّنَا لِيُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ فَٱجْعَلْ أَفْـِٔدَةً مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ تَهْوِىٓ إِلَيْهِمْ وَٱرْزُقْهُم مِّنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْكُرُونَ (٣٧)
Our Lord! I have settled part of my descendants in a barren valley, by Your sacred House, our Lord, that they may maintain the prayer. So make the hearts of a part of the people fond of them, and provide them with fruits, so that they may give thanks. (37)
رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ تَعْلَمُ مَا نُخْفِى وَمَا نُعْلِنُ ۗ وَمَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ (٣٨)
Our Lord! Indeed You know whatever we hide and whatever we disclose, and nothing is hidden from Allah on the earth or in the sky. (38)
ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى وَهَبَ لِى عَلَى ٱلْكِبَرِ إِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى لَسَمِيعُ ٱلدُّعَآءِ (٣٩)
All praise belongs to Allah, who gave me Ishmael and Isaac despite [my] old age. Indeed my Lord hears all supplications. (39)
رَبِّ ٱجْعَلْنِى مُقِيمَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِى ۚ رَبَّنَا وَتَقَبَّلْ دُعَآءِ (٤٠)
My Lord! Make me a maintainer of prayer, and my descendants [as well]. Our Lord, accept my supplication. (40)
رَبَّنَا ٱغْفِرْ لِى وَلِوَٰلِدَىَّ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلْحِسَابُ (٤١)
Our Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and all the faithful, on the day when the reckoning is held.’ (41)

(Commentary)

The verses contain a second reminder of a collection of His favors, following the first reminder contained in His saying: وَإِذْ قالَ مُوسى لِقَوْمِهِ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَةَ اللهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ أَنْجاكُمْ مِنْ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ — "When Moses said to his people, ‘Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when He delivered you from Pharaoh’s clan...’" (14:6).

Thus, the Glorified first mentioned His favor upon a group of His faithful servants, namely the Children of Israel from the progeny of Abraham; then He mentioned, secondly, His favor upon another group of them, namely the Children of Ishmael from the progeny of Abraham. This is what is contained in the supplication of Abraham (peace be upon him): رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِناً — "My Lord! Make this city a sanctuary..." (14:35) until the end of his supplication. Included therein is the favor of Allah’s granting them success (التوفيق) — the divine facilitation of means toward righteousness — to avoid the worship of idols, the favor of security in Mecca, the inclining of hearts toward its people, providing them with fruits, and other such blessings. All of this is because Allah, the Glorified, is the All-Mighty (العزيز), the All-Laudable (الحميد).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِناً — "When Abraham said, ‘My Lord! Make this city a sanctuary...’" (14:35). That is: and remember when Abraham said [this]. The demonstration "this" (هذا) refers to Mecca, may Allah the Exalted ennoble it.

Allah, the Glorified, has related a similar supplication from Abraham (peace be upon him), with some brevity, in another place with His saying: وَإِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هذا بَلَداً آمِناً وَارْزُقْ أَهْلَهُ مِنَ الثَّمَراتِ مَنْ آمَنَ مِنْهُمْ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ قالَ وَمَنْ كَفَرَ فَأُمَتِّعُهُ قَلِيلاً ثُمَّ أَضْطَرُّهُ إِلى عَذابِ النَّارِ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ — "And when Abraham said, ‘My Lord, make this a city of sanctuary, and provide its people with fruits—those of them who have faith in Allah and the Last Day.’ He said, ‘As for him who is faithless, I will let him enjoy for a little while, then I will compel him to the punishment of the Fire, and it is an evil destination.’" (2:126).

It is possible to infer from the difference in the two related expressions—namely His saying: اجْعَلْ هذا بَلَداً آمِناً — "make this a city of sanctuary" (2:126) and His saying: اجْعَلْ هَذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِناً — "Make this city a sanctuary" (14:35)—that they were two supplications he (peace be upon him) made at two different times. After he settled Ishmael and his mother in the land of Mecca and returned to the land of Palestine, he later returned to them and found that the tribe of Jurhum had turned toward neighboring them in that place. He was pleased by this and supplicated at that time, pointing to their location: رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هذا بَلَداً آمِناً — "My Lord, make this a city of sanctuary." Thus, he asked his Lord to make the place a city (بلداً) when it was not yet one, and to provide its believing people with fruits. Then, when he returned to them a time after that, he found the place to be a city, so he asked his Lord to make the city (البلد) a sanctuary (آمناً).

What supports their being two distinct supplications is the difference between them in other respects: in the verse of al-Baqarah, the supplication for the people of the city is for provision of fruits, whereas in the verses under discussion, the supplication for that is specifically for his progeny, along with other matters he prayed for on their behalf.

Accordingly, this supplication related from Abraham (peace be upon him) in these verses would be the last of what Allah the Exalted has cited in His Book of the speech and prayer of Abraham (peace be upon him). He offered it after he had settled Ishmael and his mother there, the tribe of Jurhum had neighbored them, the Sacred House (البيت الحرام) had been built, and the town of Mecca had been constructed by the hands of those residing there, as indicated by the segments of the verses.

On the assumption that the two related accounts are one single supplication, the estimation of his saying: رَبِّ اجْعَلْ — "My Lord, make..." would be: "My Lord, make this city a secure city." In one of the two verses, the object of the demonstration was omitted, and in the other, the described noun was omitted for the sake of brevity.

The intended meaning of the "security" (الأمن) he (peace be upon him) requested is legislative security (الأمن التشريعي) rather than ontological security (التكويني) — as previously mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) on the verse of al-Baqarah. He is asking his Lord to legislate for the land of Mecca the ruling of sanctity (الحرمة) and security. This is—contrary to what might be imagined—one of the greatest favors Allah has bestowed upon His servants. For if we reflect upon this divine ruling which Abraham (peace be upon him) legislated by the permission of his Lord—namely the ruling of sanctity and security—and if we contemplate the people's belief in the sacredness of this Ancient House (البيت العتيق) and the secure sanctuary of Allah surrounding it, which has been firmly rooted in their souls for four thousand years until today, we find countless religious and worldly goods and blessings returning to its people and to all the people of truth who yearn for them and whose hearts are attached to them. History has recorded much of this, and what it has not recorded is even more. Thus, His making Mecca a secure city is among the magnificent favors Allah has bestowed upon His servants.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ — "...and keep me and my children away from worshipping idols. My Lord! They have indeed misled many of the people..." until His saying: غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — "...All-forgiving, All-merciful" (14:35-36). It is said: janabahu (جنبه) and ajnabahu (أجنبه), meaning "He distanced him." His (peace be upon him) request that Allah distance him and his children from the worship of idols is a seeking of refuge and a turning to Him, the Exalted, from the misguidance he attributed to them in his saying: رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ — "My Lord! They have indeed misled..."

It is well known that this distancing and keeping away by Him, the Exalted—however it may be and whatever it may be—is a form of disposal and influence from Him, the Exalted, upon His servant in some manner. However, it is not in a manner that leads to compulsion (الإلجاء) or necessity (الاضطرار), nor does it result in coercion (القهر) or forcing (الإجبار) by stripping the attribute of choice (الاختيار) from him; for there is no merit in such a distancing that someone like Abraham, the Friend of Allah (خليل الله), would request it.

Thus, it essentially returns to what was previously mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ — "Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word..." (14:27), in that every good—whether an act or an omission—is attributed to Him, the Exalted, primarily, and then to the servant secondarily. This is in contrast to evil—whether an act or an omission—for it is attributed to the servant initially; and if it is attributed to Him, the Exalted, it is only attributed by way of retribution (المجازاة). We have already clarified this.

Therefore, the avoidance of idol worship is only realized through a distancing (إجناب) from Allah, as a mercy from Him to His servant and a special care (عناية). In reality, it is nothing but a matter the servant is clothed with and characterized by, except that he only possesses it through Allah’s granting of ownership (تمليك). Thus, He is the Owner (المالك) of it by His essence (بذاته), and the servant possesses it by His command and permission. Similarly, the servant is only guided through guidance (هداية) from Allah; there is but one guidance, but it is owned by Allah, the Glorified, by His essence, and the servant only possesses it through His granting of ownership. The most expansive word on this meaning is what occurs in the reports of the Household of Infallibility (آل العصمة): that Allah grants His servant success (يوفق) to do good and avoid evil.

In summary, the meaning of his (peace be upon him) saying: وَاجْنُبْنِي — "and keep me away" is a request for that which belongs to Allah, the Glorified, of the making (الصنع) in the servant's omission of idol worship. In other words, he is asking his Lord to preserve him and his children from worshipping idols and to guide them to the truth if they expose themselves to that, and to pour [grace] upon them if they seek it—not that He preserve them from it whether they expose themselves to it or not, or that He pour [grace] upon them whether they seek it or refuse it. This is the meaning of his (peace be upon him) supplication.

From this, it is known that the result of the supplication is for some of those prayed for, even if it is expressed in a word that encompasses all. These "some" are those prepared for it, excluding the stubborn and the arrogant among them. We shall further clarify this.

Furthermore, he (peace be upon him) offers this supplication for himself and his children: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ — "...and keep me and my children away from worshipping idols." His "children" (بنيّ) are all those who came from his lineage after him, namely the Children of Ishmael and the Children of Isaac. For just as "son" (الابن) is applied to the immediate offspring, it is likewise applied to others. The Noble Quran confirms this; the Exalted said: مِلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْراهِيمَ — "...the faith of your father, Abraham" (22:78). The term "Children of Israel" (بني إسرائيل) is repeated in reference to the Jews in over forty places in His speech, the Exalted.

Thus, he (peace be upon him) asks for distance from idol worship for himself and for all who come after him of his children, in the sense previously mentioned. Unless, of course, it is said that the circumstantial and textual indicators point to the restriction of the supplication to the progeny of Ishmael residing in the Hijaz, thus not encompassing the Children of Isaac.

Then he (peace be upon him) followed his supplication: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ with his saying: رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ — "My Lord! They have indeed misled many of the people." This is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for his supplication. He repeated the call "My Lord" (رَبِّ) to stir the divine mercy, as if to say: "I only ask You to distance me and my children from worshipping them because they have misled many people." The attribution of misguidance (الإضلال) to the idols is due to the connection that exists between misguidance and them, even if it is not a conscious connection. It is not necessary, in attributing any act or effect to a thing, that it be performed by it consciously; this is evident.

Then his (peace be upon him) saying: فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي وَمَنْ عَصانِي فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — "So whoever follows me indeed belongs to me, and as for him who disobeys me, You are indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful" (14:36) is a derivation (تفريع) from what preceded in his speech. That is: since many people were misled by idols through worshipping them, and I have sought refuge in You and presented myself and my children to You that You might keep us away from worshipping them, we and the people have split into two groups: those who are astray from the path of Your monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and those who present themselves for Your preservation and distancing. So, whoever follows me... (etc.).

In his derivation, he (peace be upon him) expressed it by saying: فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي — "So whoever follows me." Following (الاتباع) only occurs on a path (طريق) — and he also alluded to the path by his saying: أَضْلَلْنَ — "they misled," because misguidance (الضلال) only occurs away from a path. Thus, his intention by "following him" is to adhere to his religion and walk according to his conduct (سيرة), not merely to believe in the oneness of Him, the Exalted, but rather to traverse his way (طريقة) based on the monotheism of Allah, the Glorified, so that in doing so, one presents oneself to the mercy of Him, the Exalted, and His distancing from idol worship.

Evidence that the intended meaning of "following" is traversing his path is his saying in the corresponding part of his speech: وَمَنْ عَصانِي — "and as for him who disobeys me." He attributed disobedience (العصيان) to himself and did not say: "and whoever disbelieves in You" or "disobeys You" or "deviates from the truth," just as he did not say: "so whoever believes in You" or "obeys You" or "is God-conscious of You."

Thus, his intention by "following him" is traversing his path and adhering to all that he brought of belief and action; and by "disobeying him," the abandonment of his conduct and the law (الشريعة) he brought, in both belief and action. It is as if he (peace be upon him) is saying: "Whoever follows me, acts according to my law, and walks according to my conduct, he is attached to me and is among my children by way of figurative inclusion (تنزيلاً); I ask You to keep me and him away from worshipping idols. And whoever disobeys me by abandoning my way, entirely or partially—whether he be of my children or others—I do not attach him to myself, nor do I ask You for his distancing and removal; rather, I leave him between himself and Your forgiveness and mercy."

From here, several points become clear:

First: His (peace be upon him) saying: فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي وَمَنْ عَصانِي فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ is an interpretation of his saying: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ by disposing of the "children" through generalization and specification. It is like generalizing the "children" to include everyone who followed him on one hand, and specifying it by excluding those who disobeyed him from among them on the other; thus, they are not "of him" nor attached to him. In short, he (peace be upon him) attaches those who followed him after him to himself, while as for those who did not follow him, he leaves them between themselves and their Lord, the All-Forgiving, the All-Merciful, as the Exalted said: إِنَّ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِإِبْراهِيمَ لَلَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ وَهذَا النَّبِيُّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا — "Indeed the nearest of all people to Abraham are those who followed him, and this prophet and those who have faith..." (3:68).

This expansion and contraction by him (peace be upon him) is similar to the totality of what occurred between him and his Lord in the other segment of his supplication, as related in the verse of al-Baqarah: وَارْزُقْ أَهْلَهُ مِنَ الثَّمَراتِ مَنْ آمَنَ مِنْهُمْ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ قالَ وَمَنْ كَفَرَ فَأُمَتِّعُهُ قَلِيلاً ثُمَّ أَضْطَرُّهُ إِلى عَذابِ النَّارِ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ — "...and provide its people with fruits—those of them who have faith in Allah and the Last Day.’ He said, ‘As for him who is faithless, I will let him enjoy for a little while, then I will compel him to the punishment of the Fire...’" (2:126). There, he first asked for provision for the people of the city, then he specified it for those of them who believed; then Allah, the Glorified, generalized it by His saying: وَمَنْ كَفَرَ — "As for him who is faithless" secondly.

Second: It is possible to infer from his (peace be upon him) saying regarding those who followed him: "he is of me" and his silence regarding those who disobeyed him—after his supplication had been for himself and his children—that this is an adoption (تبنٍ) of everyone who followed him and an attachment of them to himself, and a negation of everyone who disobeyed him from himself, even if they were his children by birth. Or, it is an attachment of his followers to himself while remaining silent about others, based on the fact that silence is not explicit in negation.

There is no problem in this once the evidence is clear. Natural birth (الولادة الطبيعية) need not be the sole criterion for lineage (النسب) in affirmation or negation. You will not find any nation that restricts lineage in affirmation and negation to mere natural birth; rather, they continue to dispose of it through expansion and contraction. Islam also has disposals in this regard, such as the negation of the adopted son (الدعي), the child of adultery, the disbeliever, and the apostate, and the attachment of the nursed child and the child born on the bed, among others. In His speech, the Exalted, regarding the son of Noah: إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ إِنَّهُ عَمَلٌ غَيْرُ صالِحٍ — "‘He is not of your family. Indeed he is [personified] unrighteous conduct.’" (11:46).

Third: Although he (peace be upon him) did not explicitly ask for forgiveness and mercy for those who disobeyed him, but rather presented them to forgiveness and mercy by his saying: وَمَنْ عَصانِي فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ, it is not devoid of some allusion to a request for those who abandoned his way and conduct—which prepares a person for divine mercy through preservation from idol worship. This degree of disobedience does not prevent the encompassing of mercy, even if it does not necessitate it. What is intended here is not polytheism (الشرك) itself, such that it would contradict the request for forgiveness, as the Exalted said: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكا لِمَنْ يَشاءُ — "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes." (4:116).

This is the summary of what reflection upon the two noble verses yields. It is far removed from the difficulties raised by the commentators (المفسرون) regarding the aspects of the two verses, after which they went in various directions to resolve them, far from sound taste.

They first raised a difficulty regarding his (peace be upon him) saying: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ in that its apparent meaning is a request for preservation from idol worship for himself and all his children, which would be an unanswered supplication; for the Quraysh are among his children and they were pagans worshipping idols. How could someone like the Friend (peace be upon him) supplicate and then not be answered? Or how could the Exalted mention his supplication if it were idle talk (لغو) of no concern, and then not mention its rejection, contrary to the method of the Quran in all similar places? Furthermore, how could he ask for protection and infallibility (العصمة) from idol worship for himself when he is a prophet, and prophets are infallible?

It has been said in response to the difficulty of his supplication not being answered regarding his children that the intended meaning of "his children" is his immediate sons, like Ishmael, Isaac, and others, and his supplication was answered regarding them. It was also said: the intended meaning is those of his children present at the time of the supplication, and they were monotheists. It was said: Allah answered his supplication regarding some of his children and not others, and there is no deficiency in that.

It was said: the polytheists among his children did not worship idols, but rather took them as intercessors (شفعاء). It was said: they worshipped awthan (الأوثان) rather than asnam (الأصنام), and there is a difference between them: asnam are carved images (تماثيل مصورة) and awthan are uncarved images. It was said: they did not worship idols; rather, one of them would set up a stone and say, "This is a stone and the House is a stone," and he would circle around it, and they called it the dawwar (الدوار).

The fallaciousness of these views is evident. As for the first and second, they contradict the apparent meaning of the word. As for the third, the difficulty is not in a deficiency befalling the Prophet due to the non-answering of his supplication or part of it for a wisdom, but rather from the perspective of its contradiction with the method of the Quran in relating idle speech without rejecting it. As for the remaining views, the criterion for misguidance in idol worship is the polytheism of worship (شرك العبادة), which is present in all the scenarios they proposed.

It was said in response to the difficulty of a prophet asking for distancing and removal from polytheism when he is an infallible prophet: the intended meaning is steadfastness and permanence upon that. It was said: he (peace be upon him) mentioned that out of self-abasement (هضماً لنفسه) and to manifest his need for His grace, the Exalted. It was said: the intended meaning is a request for preservation from hidden polytheism (الشرك الخفي), otherwise prophets are protected from manifest polytheism.

These are weak views. As for the first, the substance of the difficulty is not resolved by it; for infallibility and protection, just as they are necessary for prophethood in its inception (حدوثاً), they are necessary for it in its continuation (بقاءً). If it were not correct for the Prophet to ask for its inception due to its necessity, it would not be correct for him to ask for its continuation for the same reason. The basis of their answer is their assumption that grace (الفيض) is separated from the Bestower (المفيض) and that the recipient (المستفيض) becomes independent in what he has received—meaning that if Allah, the Glorified, bestows something upon something, what He bestowed leaves His ownership and falls into the ownership of the recipient, and there is no meaning in asking from one who does not own. And if He, the Glorified, decrees something in its inception or continuation as a definitive decree (قضاء حتم) that does not change, then neither power nor will relates to its contrary. This is an error. For the need on the part of the recipient remains as it was before the bestowal, not differing at all; and His ownership, the Exalted, remains after the bestowal as it was before it. He, the Glorified, remains ever-powerful, able to will what He wills, even if He does not will contrary to what He has decreed as a definitive decree. Asking and requesting are effects of need (الحاجة), not effects of lack (الفقدان); so understand that. We have fully discussed this meaning in previous discussions repeatedly.

As for the second, self-abasement is only sound in non-essential matters, but not in essential ones. There is no meaning in a speaker saying, "I am not a human," intending to negate the quiddity (الماهية) out of self-abasement—unless he intends to negate perfection. The same applies to manifesting need; they do not see a need in essential, definitive matters like infallibility in prophets.

As for the third, hidden polytheism is the leaning toward and turning to other than Allah in its various degrees. Abraham (peace be upon him) justifies his saying: وَاجْنُبْنِي (etc.) by his saying: إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ (etc.). Thus, he is only asking for distancing from the worship of these idols, which is manifest polytheism, not preservation from leaning toward and turning to other than Allah, the Exalted—unless it is claimed that "idol" (الصنم) means everything one turns to other than Allah, the Glorified, and "worship" (العبادة) means absolute turning and attention; this is a claim for which there is no evidence.

Then they raised a difficulty regarding his (peace be upon him) saying: وَمَنْ عَصانِي فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ in that it includes a request for forgiveness for polytheists, while forgiveness does not relate to polytheism by the text of His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ (etc.).

It has been said in response to the difficulty: polytheism was permissible for forgiveness in previous laws (الشرائع السابقة), and it was only lifted in this law by His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ. Thus, Abraham (peace be upon him) proceeded in his supplication according to what the matter was in his law.

It was said: the intended meaning is: "and as for him who disobeys me, You are indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful to him after his repentance (توبته)"; thus, there is a deleted condition in the speech. It was said: the intended meaning is: "and as for him who disobeys me and persists in polytheism, You are indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful by transferring him from polytheism to monotheism, thus forgiving him and having mercy on him."

It was said: the intended meaning of forgiveness and mercy is the veiling (الستر) of polytheism in this world and mercy by not hastening the punishment. So the meaning is: "and as for him who disobeys me by persisting in polytheism, veil that for him and have mercy on him by delaying the punishment from him." It was said: the speech is according to its apparent meaning, and that was from him (peace be upon him) before he knew that Allah does not forgive polytheism; there is no deficiency in being ignorant of that because the forgiveness of polytheism is rationally permissible (جائزة عقلاً) and was only prevented by auditory evidence (الدليل السمعي), and it is not mandatory to know all auditory evidences in a single day. It was said: the intended meaning of "disobedience" is what is less than polytheism.

These are invalid answers. As for the first, the claim that polytheism was permissible for forgiveness in previous laws is a claim for which there is no evidence; rather, the evidence is to the contrary. Allah addressed Adam (peace be upon him) with the like of His saying—and his was the first of the previous laws: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ — "But those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]." (2:39). He related from the Messiah (peace be upon him) and his law—the last of the previous laws—his saying: إِنَّهُ مَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِ الْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْواهُ النَّارُ وَما لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ أَنْصارٍ — "...Whoever ascribes partners to Allah, Allah shall forbid him [entry into] paradise, and his abode shall be the Fire, and the wrongdoers shall not have any helpers." (5:72). Reflection upon the verses of the Resurrection, Paradise, and the Fire, and the verses of intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — and the supplications of the prophets related in the Quran leaves no doubt that for polytheism there is no salvation for its practitioner through intercession or otherwise, except through repentance before death.

As for the second, restricting forgiveness and mercy to repentance is a restriction without a restrictor; moreover, restricting disobedience to repentance spoils the correspondence in his saying: فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي وَمَنْ عَصانِي (etc.), for the repentant disobedient person returns to being among those who followed him and is attached to him (peace be upon him), so nothing remains of the correspondence except one side.

As for the third and fourth, they involve committing a violation of the apparent meaning; for the apparent meaning of requesting forgiveness for the disobedient is that Allah forgive him while he is disobedient, not that Allah forgive him after his exit through repentance from disobedience to obedience. Likewise, the apparent meaning of forgiving disobedience is the removal of the consequences of his disobedience absolutely or in the Hereafter; as for the removal of the worldly consequence only, it is a matter far from understanding.

As for the fifth, it is the most far-fetched of views. How can it be permissible to venture such a thing against the Friend (peace be upon him) when he was in the latter part of his life—as previously mentioned—that he should be ignorant of what is among the clearest of religious cognitions, then proceed upon his ignorance to intercede with his Lord for polytheists and ask forgiveness for them without seeking permission from Allah in that? Had he sought His permission, He would have informed him that such a thing cannot be. Then Allah, the Glorified, would cite in His speech what he committed of idle speech out of ignorance and not reject it by clarifying what the truth is in that. Allah, the Glorified, has excused his seeking forgiveness for his polytheist father and removed from his presence every blemish in what He said: وَما كانَ اسْتِغْفارُ إِبْراهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَنْ مَوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَها إِيَّاهُ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ أَنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ لِلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ — "Abraham’s pleading for his father’s forgiveness was only to fulfill a promise he had made him. So when it became manifest to him that he was an enemy of Allah, he repudiated him..." (9:114).

As for the sixth, restricting disobedience to what is less than polytheism is a restriction without a restrictor, unless it is formulated in a way that returns to what we have presented.

This is the sum of what the commentators mentioned in the sequel to the two verses; we have presented it in summary. They fell into what they fell into because of their neglect in investigating the meaning of His preservation, the Exalted, from polytheism, and the meaning of the derivation of his saying: فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي (etc.) from what preceded it.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبَّنا إِنِّي أَسْكَنْتُ مِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِي بِوادٍ غَيْرِ ذِي زَرْعٍ عِنْدَ بَيْتِكَ الْمُحَرَّمِ — "Our Lord! I have settled some of my descendants in a barren valley near Your Sacred House..." (14:37) until the end of the verse. مِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِي — "some of my descendants" is in the interpretation of the object of أَسْكَنْتُ — "I have settled," or it stands in its place, and the مِنْ in it is for partitivity (التبعيض). His (peace be upon him) intention by "some of my descendants" is his son Ishmael and those who would be born to him of children, not Ishmael alone, as evidenced by his saying afterward: رَبَّنا لِيُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ — "our Lord, that they may maintain the prayer."

The meaning of "barren" (غَيْرِ ذِي زَرْعٍ) is "not sown," which is more emphatic and eloquent because it indicates—as has been said—its lack of suitability for being sown due to its being stony, sandy land devoid of materials suitable for sowing. This is like His saying: قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا غَيْرَ ذِي عِوَجٍ — "an Arabic Quran, without any deviance" (39:28).

The attribution of the House to Allah, the Glorified, is because it was built for a purpose that is only suitable for Him, the Exalted, which is His worship. Its being "sacred" (المحرم) is what Allah has granted it of sanctity (الحرمة) legislatively. The adverbial phrase, namely His saying: عِنْدَ بَيْتِكَ الْمُحَرَّمِ — "near Your Sacred House," is connected to His saying: أَسْكَنْتُ — "I have settled."

This sentence of his supplication (peace be upon him)—namely His saying: رَبَّنا إِنِّي أَسْكَنْتُ until His saying: الْمُحَرَّمِ — is among the evidences for what we previously presented: that he (peace be upon him) only offered this supplication in the latter part of his life after he had built the Kaaba and people had built the town of Mecca and inhabited it. Another evidence for this is his saying: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْماعِيلَ وَإِسْحاقَ — "All praise belongs to Allah, who gave me Ishmael and Isaac despite [my] old age" (14:39).

By this, the difficulty that might be raised is repelled, where it is said: "How did he call it a 'House' and say 'I have settled some of my descendants near it' when he had not yet built it?" It is as if the questioner assumes that he only offered this prayer on the day he brought Ishmael and his mother to the land of Mecca, when it was a desolate land with no companion and no plant.

There is no need to repel it by saying that he knew through what Allah had taught him that he would build a House for Allah there, or that the House existed before that and was destroyed by some tribes or raised by Allah to the heavens during the Flood. I wonder, if these two [answers] repel this difficulty, how can they repel the difficulty that would be directed, on this assumption, toward the apparent meaning of his saying: رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِناً and the apparent meaning of his saying: وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْماعِيلَ وَإِسْحاقَ?

His saying: رَبَّنا لِيُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ — "our Lord, that they may maintain the prayer" is a clarification of his purpose for settling them there. This, in conjunction with what preceded of his saying: بِوادٍ غَيْرِ ذِي زَرْعٍ — "in a barren valley" and what follows it of his saying: فَاجْعَلْ أَفْئِدَةً مِنَ النَّاسِ تَهْوِي إِلَيْهِمْ وَارْزُقْهُمْ مِنَ الثَّمَراتِ — "so make the hearts of some people yearn for them, and provide them with fruits" (14:37), indicates that he (peace be upon him) only chose a barren valley, isolated from the provisions of life such as fresh water, green plants, delightful trees, and moderate air, and devoid of inhabitants, so that they might devote themselves entirely to the worship of Allah without being occupied by the distractions of the world.

His saying: فَاجْعَلْ أَفْئِدَةً مِنَ النَّاسِ تَهْوِي إِلَيْهِمْ (etc.) is from al-huwiyy (الهوي) in the sense of falling; that is, they yearn and incline toward them by residing with them or by performing pilgrimage (الحج) to the House and thus becoming familiar with them. "And provide them with fruits" by transporting them to them through trade, "so that they may give thanks."

His saying, the Exalted: رَبَّنا إِنَّكَ تَعْلَمُ ما نُخْفِي وَما نُعْلِنُ — "Our Lord! Indeed You know whatever we hide and whatever we disclose..." (14:38) until the end of the verse. Its meaning is clear. His saying: وَما يَخْفى عَلَى اللهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا فِي السَّماءِ — "...and nothing is hidden from Allah in the earth or in the sky" is either from the completion of Abraham’s (peace be upon him) speech or from the speech of Him, the Exalted. According to the first, in His saying: عَلَى اللهِ — "from Allah," there is a shift (التفات) whose purpose is to point to the cause of the ruling, as if it were said: "Indeed You know what we hide and what we disclose because You are Allah, from Whom nothing is hidden in the earth or in the sky." It is not far-fetched to infer from this reasoning that what is intended by "the sky" (السماء) is what is hidden from us and absent from our senses, and "the earth" (الأرض) is its opposite; so understand that.

His saying, the Exalted: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي وَهَبَ لِي عَلَى الْكِبَرِ إِسْماعِيلَ وَإِسْحاقَ إِنَّ رَبِّي لَسَمِيعُ الدُّعاءِ — "All praise belongs to Allah, who gave me Ishmael and Isaac despite [my] old age. Indeed my Lord hears all supplications" (14:39). This is like a parenthetical sentence between the segments of his supplication. He was prompted to offer it by his remembrance, within the supplications he offered, of the great favor of Allah upon him when He gave him two righteous sons like them after the ordinary causes leading to the appearance of progeny had been severed from him, and that He only gave them to him by answering his supplication for a child. So he praised Allah for what He gave them and lauded Him for answering his supplication in that.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبِّ اجْعَلْنِي مُقِيمَ الصَّلاةِ وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِي رَبَّنا وَتَقَبَّلْ دُعاءِ — "My Lord! Make me a maintainer of prayer, and some of my descendants [too]. Our Lord, accept my supplication" (14:40). The discussion regarding the attribution of his maintaining the prayer to Allah, the Glorified, is similar to the discussion regarding the attribution of His distancing him from worshipping idols; for maintaining the prayer has a relation to Him, the Exalted, by permission and will, just as it has a relation to the servant by undertaking and action. The discussion on this has already passed.

This segment is the second supplication in which he (peace be upon him) and his progeny share, and it essentially follows his first saying: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ, just as his third shared supplication is attached to it: رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوالِدَيَّ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ.
