He singled himself out in all three segments from others when he said: وَاجْنُبْنِي — "and keep me away," اجْعَلْنِي مُقِيمَ الصَّلاةِ — "Make me a maintainer of prayer," and اغْفِرْ لِي — "forgive me," because his objective was the attachment of his progeny to him, as he said in another place: وَاجْعَلْ لِي لِسانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ — "and confer on me a worthy mention among the posterity" (26:84), and in another place as Allah related by His saying: وَإِذِ ابْتَلى إِبْراهِيمَ رَبُّهُ بِكَلِماتٍ فَأَتَمَّهُنَّ قالَ إِنِّي جاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِماماً قالَ وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِي — "And when his Lord tested Abraham with certain words, and he fulfilled them, He said, ‘I am making you the Imam of mankind.’ Said he, ‘And from among my descendants?’" (2:124).

As for his saying in the first segment: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ and here: اجْعَلْنِي مُقِيمَ الصَّلاةِ وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِي, it has already been mentioned that the intended meaning of "his children" is some of them, not all of them, so the two segments correspond.

Among the correspondences of the two segments is that he emphasized his supplication in this segment with his saying: رَبَّنا وَتَقَبَّلْ دُعاءِ — "Our Lord, accept my supplication," for the request for the acceptance of the supplication is an entreaty, insistence, and emphasis, just as the reasoning in the first segment, with his saying: رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ, is in reality an emphasis of the supplication therein, with his saying: وَاجْنُبْنِي (etc.).

His saying, the Exalted: رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوالِدَيَّ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْحِسابُ — "Our Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and the faithful, on the day when the reckoning is held" (14:41). He (peace be upon him) concluded his supplication—which is the last of his supplications mentioned in the Noble Quran as previously stated—by requesting forgiveness for the believers on the Day of Resurrection. It resembles the last of what Noah (peace be upon him) prayed for of what is mentioned in the Quran: رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوالِدَيَّ وَلِمَنْ دَخَلَ بَيْتِيَ مُؤْمِناً وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ — "My Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and whoever enters my house in faith, and the faithful men and women..." (71:28).

In the verse is evidence that he (peace be upon him) was not the biological son of Azar the polytheist; for he (peace be upon him)—as you see—seeks forgiveness for his parents while he is in old age and at the end of his time. He had repudiated Azar in the early part of his time after he had sought forgiveness for him due to a promise he had made him. The Exalted said: قالَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكَ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي — "He said, ‘Peace be to you! I will plead with my Lord to forgive you...’" (19:47), and he said: وَاغْفِرْ لِأَبِي إِنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ — "and forgive my father, for he is one of those who are astray" (26:86), and He said: وَما كانَ اسْتِغْفارُ إِبْراهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَنْ مَوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَها إِيَّاهُ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ أَنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ لِلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ — "Abraham’s pleading for his father’s forgiveness was only to fulfill a promise he had made him. So when it became manifest to him that he was an enemy of Allah, he repudiated him" (9:114). The detailed discussion of his (peace be upon him) stories has already passed in Surah al-An'am in the seventh volume of the book.

Among the subtle points in his (peace be upon him) supplication is the variation in the repeated call with the words "My Lord" (رب) and "Our Lord" (ربنا), and the care in what he added to himself regarding what is specific to himself of precedence and leadership (الإمامة), and in what he added to himself and others regarding shared matters.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Nu'aym narrated in al-Dala'il from 'Aqil ibn Abi Talib: that when the six individuals from the Ansar came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—he sat with them at the Jamrah of al-'Aqabah—he called them to Allah and to His worship and to support his religion. They asked him to present to them what had been revealed to him. So he recited from Surah Ibrahim: وَإِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِناً — وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ until the end of the Surah. The people were moved to tears—and they became humble when they heard from him what they heard, and they answered him.

In Tafsir al-'Ayyashi, from Abu 'Ubaydah from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "Whoever loves us, he is of us, the People of the House (أهل البيت)." I said: "May I be your ransom, of you?" He said: "Of us, by Allah! Have you not heard the saying of Allah, and it is the saying of Abraham (peace be upon him): فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي — 'So whoever follows me indeed belongs to me'?"

In the same source, from Muhammad al-Halabi from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: "Whoever among you is God-conscious of Allah and acts righteously, he is of us, the People of the House." He said: "Of you, the People of the House?" He said: "Of us, the People of the House. Abraham said regarding it: فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي." 'Umar ibn Yazid said: I said to him: "Of the Progeny of Muhammad (آل محمد)?" He said: "Yes, by Allah, of the Progeny of Muhammad; yes, by Allah, of their very selves. Do you not hear the saying of Allah the Exalted: إِنَّ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِإِبْراهِيمَ — لَلَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ — 'Indeed the nearest of all people to Abraham are those who followed him' and the saying of Abraham: فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي?"

I say: It has been mentioned in some narrations that the Children of Ishmael never worshipped an idol at all, following the supplication of Abraham: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ, and that they only said: "These are our intercessors with Allah." It appears that the narration is fabricated (موضوعة), and this was previously alluded to in the preceding commentary.

Likewise, what has been mentioned in some narrations from both the Sunni and Shi'a chains: that the land of Ta'if was in Jordan in the land of Palestine, and when Abraham (peace be upon him) supplicated for his children with his saying: وَارْزُقْهُمْ مِنَ الثَّمَراتِ, Allah commanded it, and it traveled with its soil to Mecca, performed seven circuits around the House, and then settled where Ta'if is now.

This, although possible in itself by way of miracle (الإعجاز), such weak and disconnected (مرسلة) narrations are not sufficient to establish it. Moreover, these verses are in the position of mentioning favors (الامتنان), and had this supplication and Allah’s answering of it been accompanied by such a magnificent, wondrous sign and clear miracle, it would have been alluded to as an additional favor. And Allah knows best.

In the disconnected narration of al-'Ayyashi, from Hariz from one who mentioned it from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them): that he used to recite: "My Lord, forgive me and my two sons" (رب اغفر لي ولولدي), meaning: Ishmael and Isaac. In his other disconnected narration from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): the like of it. The apparent meaning of this narration is that the recitation is based on the disbelief of Abraham’s father. The two narrations are weak and are not to be given weight.

Ayahs

وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَـٰفِلًا عَمَّا يَعْمَلُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ ۚ إِنَّمَا يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ لِيَوْمٍ تَشْخَصُ فِيهِ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ (٤٢)
Do not suppose that Allah is oblivious of what the wrongdoers are doing. He is only granting them respite until the day when the eyes will be glazed. (42)
مُهْطِعِينَ مُقْنِعِى رُءُوسِهِمْ لَا يَرْتَدُّ إِلَيْهِمْ طَرْفُهُمْ ۖ وَأَفْـِٔدَتُهُمْ هَوَآءٌ (٤٣)
Scrambling with their heads upturned, there will be a fixed gaze in their eyes, and their hearts will be vacant. (43)
وَأَنذِرِ ٱلنَّاسَ يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ فَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ أَخِّرْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ نُّجِبْ دَعْوَتَكَ وَنَتَّبِعِ ٱلرُّسُلَ ۗ أَوَلَمْ تَكُونُوٓا۟ أَقْسَمْتُم مِّن قَبْلُ مَا لَكُم مِّن زَوَالٍ (٤٤)
Warn the people of the day when the punishment will overtake them, whereat the wrongdoers will say, ‘Our Lord! Respite us for a short time so that we may respond to Your call and follow the apostles.’ [They will be told,] ‘Did you not use to swear earlier that there would be no reverse for you, (44)
وَسَكَنتُمْ فِى مَسَـٰكِنِ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَتَبَيَّنَ لَكُمْ كَيْفَ فَعَلْنَا بِهِمْ وَضَرَبْنَا لَكُمُ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ (٤٥)
while you dwelt in the dwellings of those who had wronged themselves [before], and it had been made clear to you how We had dealt with them [before you], and We had [also] cited examples for you?’ (45)
وَقَدْ مَكَرُوا۟ مَكْرَهُمْ وَعِندَ ٱللَّهِ مَكْرُهُمْ وَإِن كَانَ مَكْرُهُمْ لِتَزُولَ مِنْهُ ٱلْجِبَالُ (٤٦)
They certainly devised their plots, but their plots are known to Allah, and their plots are not such as to dislodge the mountains. (46)
فَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِفَ وَعْدِهِۦ رُسُلَهُۥٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ ذُو ٱنتِقَامٍ (٤٧)
So do not suppose that Allah will break His promise to His apostles. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, avenger. (47)
يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ ٱلْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ ۖ وَبَرَزُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلْوَٰحِدِ ٱلْقَهَّارِ (٤٨)
The day when the earth is turned into another earth and the heavens [as well], and they are presented before Allah, the One, the All-paramount (48)
وَتَرَى ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ يَوْمَئِذٍ مُّقَرَّنِينَ فِى ٱلْأَصْفَادِ (٤٩)
—on that day you will see the guilty bound together in chains, (49)
سَرَابِيلُهُم مِّن قَطِرَانٍ وَتَغْشَىٰ وُجُوهَهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ (٥٠)
their garments made of pitch, and the Fire covering their faces, (50)
لِيَجْزِىَ ٱللَّهُ كُلَّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٥١)
so that Allah may reward every soul for what it has earned. Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning. (51)
هَـٰذَا بَلَـٰغٌ لِّلنَّاسِ وَلِيُنذَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَلِيَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا هُوَ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ وَلِيَذَّكَّرَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٥٢)
This is a proclamation for mankind, so that they may be warned thereby and know that He is indeed the One God, and those who possess intellect may take admonition. (52)

(Commentary)

When the Glorified has finished warning and giving glad tidings in the preceding verses, and has called toward His path insofar as He is the All-Mighty (العزيز), the All-Laudable (الحميد), He concludes His exposition by dispelling what might occur to the imaginations of the weak-minded among people: that if the matter were as described, and if this call were a prophetic summons from an All-Mighty and All-Laudable Lord, then why do these wrongdoers (الظالمين) enjoy whatever they desire? Why does He not seize the wrongdoers for their injustice, nor curb those who hold back from His call and oppose His command? Is He in a state of heedlessness (غفلة) regarding what they do, or has He failed His promise (مخلف وعده) to His messengers—promising them victory and then not fulfilling His word?

The Exalted answered that He is not heedless of what the wrongdoers do, nor does He fail His promise to His messengers. How could He, when He is All-Knowing of what they scheme, and He is All-Mighty, Possessor of Retribution (ذو انتقام)? Rather, He only delays them for a severe day, which is the Day of Recompense. Furthermore, He may seize them for their sins in this world, just as He seized the nations of the past.

Then He concludes the Surah with His saying: هذا بَلاغٌ لِلنَّاسِ وَلِيُنْذَرُوا بِهِ وَلِيَعْلَمُوا أَنَّما هُوَ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ وَلِيَذَّكَّرَ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ "This is a proclamation for mankind, so that they may be warned thereby, and know that He is but one God, and so that those who possess intellect may take admonition." (14:52). This is a comprehensive verse that encompasses the purpose of the Surah, as its explanation will follow, Allah willing.

(12 — al-Mizan — 6)

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ اللهَ غافِلاً عَمَّا يَعْمَلُ الظَّالِمُونَ "Do not suppose that Allah is oblivious of what the wrongdoers are doing..." (14:42) until the end of the two verses. It is said: "his sight became fixed (شخص بصره)" meaning it became still such that his eyelid does not blink. A camel is said to be muhti’ (مهطع) when it lowers its neck or raises it; hata‘a (هطع) and ahta‘a (أهطع) carry the same meaning. It is said: "he craned his head (أقنع رأسه)" when he raises it. His saying: لا يرتد إليهم طرفهم "their gaze not returning to them" means they are unable to blink due to the horror of what they witness. His saying: وأفئدتهم هواء "and their hearts are empty" means their hearts are void of intellect and planning due to the severity of the standing, or that they have vanished.

The meaning is: Do not reckon Allah, nor think Him heedless of what these wrongdoers do—despite what you witness of their enjoyment, their luxury in living, and their corruption in the earth. Allah only grants them respite and delays their punishment until a day in which their eyes shall be fixed and will not blink, while they are craning their necks, raising their heads, unable to return their gaze, and their hearts are stunned, void of any artifice or planning due to the intensity of the horror of the Day of Resurrection. In the verse is a warning for the wrongdoers and a consolation for others.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْذِرِ النَّاسَ يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمُ الْعَذابُ "Warn mankind of the day when the punishment will come to them..." (14:44) until the end of the verse. In the verse is a warning after a warning, and between the two warnings is a difference from two aspects: First, the warning in the two preceding verses is a warning of the horrors Allah has prepared for the Day of Resurrection and the painful punishment therein. As for what is in this verse and what follows it, it is a warning of the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) — the total annihilation of a people — in this world. Evidence for this is His saying: فَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا رَبَّنا أَخِّرْنا إِلى أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ "...Then the wrongdoers will say, ‘Our Lord! Grant us respite for a short time...’" (14:44) etc.

By this, it becomes clear that there is no basis for what some have mentioned—that the meaning of "this day" in which the punishment comes to them is the Day of Resurrection; nor what others mentioned—that it refers to the day of death.

The second aspect: The first warning is a warning of a definitive punishment that cannot be averted from a wrongdoing nation or a wrongdoing individual within a nation. As for the second warning, it is a warning of a punishment that is not averted from a wrongdoing nation, but as for the individual, it might be averted from him. Therefore, you see that the Exalted says first: وَأَنْذِرِ النَّاسَ "Warn mankind," then He says: فَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا "Then the wrongdoers will say," and He did not say: "Then they [the people] will say," because the punishment of extirpation does not strike the believers. The Exalted said: ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كَذلِكَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نُنْجِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "Then We shall deliver Our apostles and those who have faith. Thus it is a duty upon Us to deliver the faithful." (10:103). It only strikes the wrongdoing nation upon the arrival of their appointed term, and they are a group of the wrongdoers of the nation, not all its individuals.

In short, His saying: وَأَنْذِرِ النَّاسَ يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمُ الْعَذابُ is a warning to the people of the punishment of extirpation which cuts off the roots of the wrongdoers among them. It has already been mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah Yunus and elsewhere that this is decreed for the nations as a judgment between them and their messenger, even for this Muhammadan nation. This threat has been repeated by the Exalted in several places in His speech.

This is the day in which Allah purifies the earth from the filth of polytheism (الشرك) and injustice (الظلم), and on that day, none shall be worshipped upon it except Allah, the Glorified. For the call is universal and the "nation" refers to the inhabitants of the earth; so when Allah erases polytheism from them, none shall remain among them except the believers, and the religion (الدين) will be entirely for Allah. The Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنا فِي الزَّبُورِ مِنْ بَعْدِ الذِّكْرِ أَنَّ الْأَرْضُ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ "Certainly We wrote in the Psalms, after the Torah: ‘Indeed My righteous servants shall inherit the earth.’" (21:105).

From what has preceded, the answer becomes clear to the objection raised against the punishment in the verse being the punishment of extirpation—namely, that the restriction (القصر) in the previous verse contradicts it; for His saying: إِنَّما يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ لِيَوْمٍ تَشْخَصُ فِيهِ الْأَبْصارُ "He only respites them until the day when the eyes will be glazed" (14:42) restricts their seizure and punishment to the Day of Resurrection.

This is because you have known that the punishment warned of in the two preceding verses is the punishment that no averter can turn away from them, and from which no wrongdoer can escape, and it is restricted to the punishment of the Day of Resurrection. Its being confined to the Day of Resurrection does not contradict the existence of another type of punishment in this world.

Furthermore, if the restriction were established as the objector desires, it would negate what is indicated by numerous verses pointing to the descent of punishment upon this nation, as we have alluded.

Moreover, interpreting the punishment in the verse as the punishment of the Day of Resurrection would necessitate turning the verses away from their apparent meaning (ظهورها) and abandoning what the context (السياق) provides, for which there is no justification.

His saying: فَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا رَبَّنا أَخِّرْنا إِلى أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ نُجِبْ دَعْوَتَكَ وَنَتَّبِعِ الرُّسُلَ "...Then the wrongdoers will say, ‘Our Lord! Grant us respite for a short time, so that we may respond to Your call and follow the apostles.’" (14:44). What is meant by it is the wrongdoers among the people, and they are those whom the extirpating punishment seizes and does not bypass. Their intent in saying: أَخِّرْنا إِلى أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ is a request for respite for a short duration to be added to their lifespan in the world so they may rectify what they missed through their wrongdoing. The evidence for this is their saying: نُجِبْ دَعْوَتَكَ وَنَتَّبِعِ الرُّسُلَ "so that we may respond to Your call and follow the apostles."

The expression "the apostles" (الرسل) in the plural in their saying: وَنَتَّبِعِ الرُّسُلَ, even though the verse apparently describes the state of the wrongdoers of this nation—which would normally require saying "and follow the Messenger"—is only to indicate that the criterion in the descent of this punishment is the judgment between the messengership and its deniers, without being specific to one messenger over another, as suggested by His saying: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ "There is a messenger for every nation: so when their messenger comes, judgment is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged." (10:47).

His saying: أَوَلَمْ تَكُونُوا أَقْسَمْتُمْ مِنْ قَبْلُ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ زَوالٍ "‘Did you not use to swear before that there will be no decline for you?" (14:44). Swearing (الإقسام) is the linking of a judgment in speech to a noble matter by virtue of its nobility to indicate its truthfulness; for if the speaker lied while having sworn in his speech, he would thereby strip the nobility from that by which he swore—such as our saying: "By Allah, such is the case," or "By my life, the matter is thus." Swearing is considered the strongest means of emphasis (التأكيد). It is not unlikely that the swearing in the verse is a metaphor (كناية) for presenting the speech in a decisive form that does not accept hesitation.

The speech is based on an implied "it is said," and the meaning is that it is said to them by way of rebuke and reprimand: Did you not use to swear before the descent of the punishment that there would be no decline for you, and that with the power, might, and means of defense you possessed, you were an eternal nation dominant over events? So why do you now ask for respite for a short time?

His saying, the Exalted: وَسَكَنْتُمْ فِي مَساكِنِ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ "And you dwelt in the dwellings of those who had wronged themselves..." (14:45) until the end of the verse, is coordinated (معطوف) with the position of His saying: أَقْسَمْتُمْ in the previous verse. The meaning is: Did you not dwell in the dwellings of those who wronged themselves among the previous nations? And it became clear to you that this call is true and that its rejection is followed by an extirpating punishment, from two aspects: the aspect of observation (المشاهدة), as it became clear to you how We dealt with those wrongdoers in whose dwellings you resided; and the aspect of exposition (البيان), as We struck parables for you and warned you of an extirpating punishment that follows the denial of truth and the rejection of the prophetic call, cutting off the roots of the wrongdoers.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَد مَكَرُوا مَكْرَهُمْ وَعِنْدَ اللهِ مَكْرُهُمْ وَإِنْ كانَ مَكْرُهُمْ لِتَزُولَ مِنْهُ الْجِبالُ "They indeed devised their stratagems, and their stratagems were with Allah, even if their stratagems were such as to make mountains move." (14:46). This is a circumstantial clause (حال) from the pronoun in فَعَلْنا "We dealt" in the previous verse, or from the pronoun in بِهِمْ "with them" therein, or from both pronouns together as has been said. The plural pronouns refer back to "those who wronged" (الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا).

The meaning of their stratagem (مكرهم) being "with Allah" is His encompassing it with His knowledge and power. It is well known that a stratagem (المكر) — a deceptive plot — is only a stratagem if the one plotted against does not encompass it and is ignorant of it. But if the one plotted against is aware of what the plotter has prepared and is capable of repelling it, the stratagem becomes futile or returns as a plot against the plotter himself, as the Exalted said: وَما يَمْكُرُونَ إِلَّا بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ وَما يَشْعُرُونَ "...and they do not plot except against themselves, but they do not perceive." (6:123).

His saying: وَإِنْ كانَ مَكْرُهُمْ لِتَزُولَ مِنْهُ الْجِبالُ — the particle in (إِنْ) is conjunctive (وصلية), as has been said, and the lam (اللام) in لِتَزُولَ is connected to an implied word indicated by the term "stratagem," such as our saying "necessitates" or "requires" and the like. The estimation is: Allah encompasses their stratagem, knowing it and capable of repelling it, whether their stratagem was less than this intensity or whether it reached this intensity.

The meaning is: It became clear to you how We dealt with them while they had plotted the utmost of their stratagems, and Allah was encompassing their plot, even if their plot was so great as to necessitate the removal of mountains.

It has also been said that in (إِنْ) is negative (نافية) and the lam is that which enters upon the negated, and what is meant by "mountains" are the signs (الآيات) and miracles (المعجزات) as a metaphor. The meaning would be: Their stratagem was not such as to nullify the signs of Allah and His miracles, which are like firm mountains that do not move from their place. This meaning was supported by the reading of Ibn Mas'ud (وما كان مكرهم), but it is a remote meaning.

It has also been read: لَتَزُولَ with a fathah on the first lam and a dammah on the second lam. According to this, in (إِنْ) is the lightened form of the emphasized (مخففة من المشددة), and the meaning is: And the truth is that their stratagem was of such greatness that the mountains would move because of it.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ اللهَ مُخْلِفَ وَعْدِهِ رُسُلَهُ إِنَّ اللهَ عَزِيزٌ ذُو انتِقامٍ "So do not suppose that Allah will break His promise to His apostles. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, avenger." (14:47). This is a derivation (تفريع) from what preceded—that the leaving of the wrongdoers for their deeds is only to delay them until the Day of Resurrection. That is, if the matter is so, then do not reckon Allah as failing what He promised His messengers of victory and the seizing of those who hold back from their call. And how could He fail His promise when He is All-Mighty, Possessor of severe Retribution? It is a necessary consequence of His absolute Might (عزته المطلقة) that He does not fail His promise; for failing a promise is either due to the promiser being unable to fulfill what he promised, or due to a change of mind caused by a second state that overcomes him contrary to what the first state—which necessitated the promise—incited him toward. But Allah, the Glorified, is All-Mighty in the absolute sense; He is not described by incapacity, nor does any state or anything else overcome Him, and He is the One, the All-Paramount (الواحد القهار).

It is also a necessary consequence of His being described with retribution (الانتقام) that He takes retribution for the truth from those who were arrogant toward it and exalted themselves over it, and He exacts justice for the oppressed from the oppressor.

"Possessor of Retribution" (ذو انتقام) is among His Beautiful Names (أسمائه الحسنى) by which Allah has named Himself in several places in His speech, and in all of them, He paired it with His name "the All-Mighty" (العزيز). The Exalted said: وَاللهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو انْتِقامٍ "and Allah is all-mighty, avenger" (3:4, 5:95), and He said: أَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِعَزِيزٍ ذِي انْتِقامٍ "Is not Allah an all-mighty avenger?" (39:37), and He said in the verse under discussion: إِنَّ اللهَ عَزِيزٌ ذُو انتِقامٍ. From this, it appears that "Possessor of Retribution" is one of the branches of the name "the All-Mighty."

(A Discourse on the Meaning of Retribution and its Attribution to the Exalted)

Retribution (الانتقام) is punishment (العقوبة), but not just any punishment; rather, it is a specific punishment, which is to make another taste an amount of evil equivalent to what they made you taste, or more. The Exalted said: فَمَنِ اعْتَدى عَلَيْكُمْ فَاعْتَدُوا عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا اعْتَدى عَلَيْكُمْ وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ "...So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you, and be wary of Allah..." (2:194).

It is a vital principle practiced by humans, and sometimes actions resembling it are observed from some animals as well. Whatever the case, the purpose that incites a human toward it varies. The motive for individual retribution is usually seeking healing (التشفي) — the relief of one's inner anger or pain. When one person deprives another of some good or makes them taste some evil, the one to whom this was done finds in himself such grief and regret that its flare does not settle nor its fire extinguish except by making the other taste an evil equivalent to what he tasted, or more. Thus, the factor that calls for it is internal sensation (الإحساس الباطني); as for the intellect (العقل), it may sometimes permit and execute it, and sometimes recoil from it.

Social retribution (الانتقام الاجتماعي)—by which we mean legal retaliations (القصاصات) and the types of penalties we find in the customs and laws circulating in societies, whether advanced or primitive—is predominantly motivated by an intellectual goal and a rational objective, which is the preservation of the system from disorder and the blocking of the path to chaos and turmoil. Were it not for the principle of retribution and the penalizing of the criminal offender for what he committed and trespassed, public security would be disrupted and peace would depart from among the people.

Therefore, this type of retribution is one of the rights of society, even if it sometimes carries an individual right—such as one who wrongs another in a way that involves legal penalty. The wrongdoer may be penalized to fulfill the right of society even if the wronged party waives his right through forgiveness.

Thus, it has become clear that some retribution is based on sensation—which is individual retribution whose goal is healing—and some is based on the intellect—which is social retribution whose goal is the preservation of the system. This latter is among the rights of society, or if you wish, you could say it is among the rights of the established custom (السنة) or law (القانون) in the society. For the uprightness of the regulations that moderate human life and their inherent soundness require the penalizing of the criminal who deviates from them and making him taste the bitter recompense of his evil deed. Thus, it is among the rights of the custom and law just as it is among the rights of society.

Once you know this, you will realize that what is attributed to the Exalted in the Book and the Sunnah regarding retribution is that which is a right among the rights of the Divine Religion (الدين الإلهي) and the Heavenly Law (الشريعة السماوية). If you wish, say it is among the rights of the Islamic society, even if it sometimes carries an individual right when the Glorified exacts justice for the oppressed from his oppressor; for He is the Guardian (الولي), the All-Laudable (الحميد).

As for individual retribution based on sensation for the purpose of healing, His Sacred Presence is too exalted to be harmed by the crimes of the criminals and the disobedience of the sinners, or to be benefited by the obedience of the doers of good.

From here, the invalidity of the objection raised by some becomes apparent—that retribution is only for the healing of the heart, and since the Exalted is neither benefited nor harmed by any of the deeds of His servants, whether good or evil, obedience or disobedience, there is no basis for attributing retribution to Him. Furthermore, they argue that His infinite mercy refuses to punish them with an eternal, infinite punishment; for how could it be otherwise when even a possessor of mercy among humans pities a criminal who commits any sin if it was out of ignorance, and He describes the human—who is His creature whose state is known to Him—with that, saying: إِنَّهُ كانَ ظَلُوماً جَهُولاً "...Indeed he is most unfair and senseless." (33:72).

The reason for its invalidity is that it contains a confusion between individual and social retribution. That which is established for the Exalted is the social type, not the individual as imagined. It also contains a confusion between psychological mercy (الرحمة النفسانية)—which is an affection and a cardiac reaction in the human—and rational mercy (الرحمة العقلية)—which is the completion of the deficiency of the deficient who is prepared for that. That which is established for the Exalted is rational mercy, not psychological mercy. Eternity in punishment is only established when the capacity for mercy and the possibility of emanation (الإفاضة) are nullified. The Exalted said: بَلى مَنْ كَسَبَ سَيِّئَةً وَأَحاطَتْ بِهِ خَطِيئَتُهُ فَأُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ "Certainly whoever earns misdeeds and is besieged by his iniquity—such shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they shall remain in it [forever]." (2:81).

There is a subtle point here that you must be mindful of: the meaning of retribution attributed to the Exalted that preceded only follows the path of recompense, reward, and punishment. As for the path of the "results of deeds" (نتائج الأعمال), its reality returns to the attachment of painful, evil forms to the human soul arising from the wicked traits (الملكات) it acquired in the life of this world, after death. The discussion regarding the recompense of deeds has already preceded in the first volume of the book under His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَسْتَحْيِي أَنْ يَضْرِبَ مَثَلاً ما "Indeed Allah is not ashamed to draw a parable..." (2:26).

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَالسَّماواتُ وَبَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ "The day when the earth is transformed into another earth and the heavens [as well], and they emerge before Allah, the One, the All-paramount." (14:48). The adverbial phrase (الظرف) is connected to His saying: ذُو انتِقامٍ "Avenger." The specification of His retribution to the Day of Resurrection, despite its generality to all times and circumstances, is only because that day is the highest manifestation of Divine retribution, just as the specification of their emerging before Allah to that day is likewise. Following this pattern are most of the descriptions mentioned in His speech regarding the Day of Resurrection, such as His saying: الْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ "All command that day will belong to Allah" (82:19) and His saying: ما لَكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ عاصِمٍ "...you will have no defender against Allah..." (40:33), and so on, as has been alluded to repeatedly.

It is apparent that the lam (اللام) in "the earth" (الأرض) is for definite reference (للعهد) in both instances, and likewise in "the heavens" (السماوات). "The heavens" is coordinated with the first "earth." The meaning is: this earth will be transformed into other than this earth, and these heavens will be transformed into other than these heavens.

The commentators (المفسرين) have various opinions regarding the meaning of the transformation of the earth and the heavens: It was said that the earth will be transformed into silver and the heavens into gold; and it was sometimes said that the earth will be transformed from a pure earth like silver, and the heavens likewise.

It was said: the earth will be transformed into fire and the heavens into gardens.

It was said: the earth will be transformed into a pure loaf of bread from which people will eat throughout the Day of Resurrection.

It was said: the earth will be transformed for every group according to what their state necessitates; so for some believers, it will be transformed into a loaf of bread from which they eat as long as they are in the arenas (العرصات), and for others into silver, and for the disbeliever into fire.

It was said: the transformation is that the earth will be increased and decreased, its hills, mountains, valleys, and trees will vanish, and it will be stretched like the stretching of leather and become level, such that you see therein neither curve nor mound. The heavens will change by the departure of the sun, the moon, and the stars. In short, each of the earth and the heavens will change from the attributes and forms they possess in the world.

The source of their disagreement in interpreting the transformation is the variation in the narrations (الروايات) mentioned in the commentary of the verse. However, even if the narrations were authentic and continuous, their variation would be the strongest evidence that their literal meaning is not intended, and that their statements are positioned as parables for the sake of approximation (التمثيل للتقريب).

Sufficient reflection on the verses that revolve around the transformation of the earth and the heaven yields that the matter of transformation is greater than what you might imagine of leveling a mountain onto a plain or transforming dust into silver or a pure loaf of bread, for example. Such as His saying, the Exalted: وَأَشْرَقَتِ الْأَرْضُ بِنُورِ رَبِّها "The earth will get bright with the light of its Lord..." (39:69), and His saying: وَسُيِّرَتِ الْجِبالُ فَكانَتْ سَراباً "the mountains will be set in motion and become a mirage" (78:20), and His saying: وَتَرَى الْجِبالَ تَحْسَبُها جامِدَةً وَهِيَ تَمُرُّ مَرَّ السَّحابِ "You see the mountains, which you suppose to be stationary, while they drift like clouds..." (27:88)—if the verse is looking toward the Day of Resurrection—among other verses.

The verses inform of a system other than this system we are familiar with, and affairs beyond what we imagine. For the brightening of the earth on that day with the light of its Lord is different from the brightening of its surface with the light of the sun, the stars, or anything else. The setting of mountains in motion usually ends in their removal from their place and their disintegration, for example, not in their becoming a mirage. Thus, we hope that Allah, the Glorified, will grant us success to expand the discourse on this meaning in what is to come, Allah willing.

His saying: وَبَرَزُوا لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ — the meaning of their emerging (بروزهم) and appearing before Allah on that day—despite things being always manifest and not hidden from Him—is the falling away of all causes (العلل) and means (الأسباب) that used to veil them from the Exalted as long as they were in the world. On that day—according to what they witness—nothing of the causes will remain that possesses them, takes charge of their affair, or is independent in affecting them except Allah, the Glorified, as indicated by many verses. They will not turn to any side nor face any direction in their outward or inward, their present or their past and absent states and deeds, except that they find Him, the Glorified, a witness (شاهداً) dominant (مهيمناً) over it and encompassing (محيطاً) it.

The evidence for what we have mentioned is His being described as "the One, the All-Paramount" (الواحد القهار), which suggests a type of dominance. Their emerging before Allah on that day only arises from His being the One upon Whom the existence of everything subsists, and Who subdues everyone besides Him among those who might affect; thus no barrier comes between them and Him, and they are manifest before Him with an absolute emergence.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَرَى الْمُجْرِمِينَ يَوْمَئِذٍ مُقَرَّنِينَ فِي الْأَصْفادِ سَرابِيلُهُمْ مِنْ قَطِرانٍ وَتَغْشى وُجُوهَهُمُ النَّارُ "On that day you will see the guilty bound together in chains. Their garments will be of pitch, and the Fire will envelop their faces." (14:48-49). Muqarranin (مقرنين) is from taqrin, which is the joining of a thing to its counterpart. Asfad (أصفاد) is the plural of safad, which is the shackle (الغل) that joins the hand to the neck, or it is the chain (السلسلة) in general that joins those who are bound. Sarabīl (سرابيل) is the plural of sirbāl, which is the shirt (القميص). Qatiran (قطران) is a black, foul-smelling substance with which camels are smeared; they are smeared with it until it becomes like a shirt upon them. Ghashawah (غشاوة) with a fathah is veiling and covering. It is said: ghashiya yaghsha ghashawatan, meaning he veiled and covered it. The meaning of the two verses is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيَجْزِيَ اللهُ كُلَّ نَفْسٍ ما كَسَبَتْ إِنَّ اللهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ "That Allah may reward every soul for what it has earned. Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning." (14:51). The meaning of the verse is clear. In its apparent sense, it indicates that what every soul is recompensed with is the very thing it earned of good or evil, even if its form has changed. Thus, it is among the verses indicating that what attaches to them on the Day of Resurrection is the result of their deeds.

The verse explains, first, the meaning of recompense (الجزاء) on the Day of Recompense; and second, the meaning of His retribution on that day—that it is not of the nature of punishing the criminal sinner to seek healing from him, but rather the attachment of what the deed of the criminal requires to him. If you wish, say: the delivery of exactly what the criminal earned to him.

In the justification of this recompense—which is on the Day of Resurrection—with His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ "Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning," there is an allusion that the recompense occurs without separation or delay, except that the time of its manifestation is that day and no other. Or, that the judgment of recompense and its recording occur at the time of the deed, and its realization is on the Day of Resurrection. The outcome of the two views is one in reality.

His saying, the Exalted: هذا بَلاغٌ لِلنَّاسِ وَلِيُنْذَرُوا بِهِ وَلِيَعْلَمُوا أَنَّما هُوَ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ وَلِيَذَّكَّرَ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ "This is a proclamation for mankind, so that they may be warned thereby, and know that He is but one God, and so that those who possess intellect may take admonition." (14:52). Balagh (بَلاغٌ) means "conveyance" (تبليغ) according to what al-Raghib mentioned, or "sufficiency" (كفاية) according to what others mentioned.

The verse is the conclusion of the Surah, so it is most appropriate that the demonstration with "this" (هذا) refers to the exposition presented in the Surah, not to the entirety of the Quran as some mentioned, nor to what was mentioned from His saying: وَلا تَحْسَبَنَّ اللهَ غافِلاً عَمَّا يَعْمَلُ الظَّالِمُونَ until the end of the Surah as others mentioned.

His saying: وَلِيُنْذَرُوا بِهِ etc.—the lam therein is for the purpose (للغاية), and it is coordinated with a deleted part that was only deleted due to the magnitude of its affair and the greatness of its status, which the understandings of people cannot encompass because it contains Divine secrets beyond their capacity. Their intellects only accommodate what was mentioned of its purposes, which are: warning, knowledge of His Oneness, and admonition. Thus, they are warned by what was mentioned therein of His seizing the wrongdoers sooner or later; the proof is completed against them by what was mentioned therein of the signs of monotheism (التوحيد); and the believers among them specifically take admonition by what it contains of Divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية).

By this, the conclusion of the Surah corresponds with its opening—namely, His saying at the beginning of the Surah: كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ إِلَيْكَ لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهمْ إِلى صِراطِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ "[This is] a Book We have sent down to you that you may bring mankind out from darkness into light by the command of their Lord, to the path of the All-mighty, the All-laudable" (14:1). It has already preceded that the significance of the verse is the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to call and convey to the path of Allah insofar as He is their All-Mighty, All-Laudable Lord, to bring people out from darkness into light by His permission. For if they respond to the call and believe, they have thereby actually come out from the darkness of disbelief into the light of faith. And if they do not respond, they are warned and made aware of the true monotheism, and they come out from ignorance into knowledge—which is a type of coming out from darkness into light, even if it is a burden and a loss for them. In the call—in any case—is a warning for people, an informing of them that He is but one God, and an admonition for those who possess intellect among them specifically, and they are the believers.

(A Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of transmission from Thawban: A Jew came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "O Muhammad!" Thawban pushed him with his leg and said: "Say: O Messenger of Allah!" He replied: "I do not call him except by the names of his family." He said: "Have you considered the saying of Allah: يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَالسَّماواتُ 'The day when the earth is transformed into another earth and the heavens [as well]'—where will the people be on that day?" He said: "في الظلمة دون المحشر" — "In the darkness before the Gathering." He asked: "What is the first thing the people of Paradise will eat when they enter it?" He said: "كبد الحوت" — "The liver of the fish." He asked: "What will be their drink following that?" He said: "السلسبيل" — "Salsabil." He said: "You have spoken the truth, O Muhammad."

I say: The hadith is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Muslim, Ibn Jarir, al-Hakim, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il from Thawban: similar to it up to his saying: "In the darkness." It is also narrated from several sources from Aisha: that she asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about that, and he said: "على الصراط" — "Upon the Bridge."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Thuwayr ibn Abi Fakhtah from al-Husayn ibn Ali (عليه‌السلام), he said: "تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ means into an earth upon which sins have not been earned, manifest—there are no mountains nor vegetation upon it, just as He spread it the first time."

I say: al-Qummi also narrated it in his Tafsir, and in it is an indication of the occurrence of mountains and likewise vegetation after the completion of the creation of the earth.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bazzar, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Ba'th extracted from Ibn Mas'ud, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said regarding the saying of Allah: يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ. He said: "أرض بيضاء كأنها فضة لم يسفك فيها دم حرام ـ ولم تعمل فيها خطيئة" — "A white earth as if it were silver, in which no forbidden blood has been shed, and no sin has been committed."

I say: It was also narrated from Ibn Mardawayh from Ali from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): similar to it.

In it, Ibn Abi al-Dunya in Sifat al-Jannah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim extracted from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding the verse, he said: "The earth will be transformed from silver and the heaven from gold."

I say: Some have interpreted the speech as a comparison (التشبيه), as occurred in the previous hadith of Ibn Mas'ud.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام), he said: Abrash al-Kalbi asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ. He said: "تبدل خبزة نقية يأكل الناس منها حتى يفرغ من الحساب" — "It will be transformed into a pure loaf of bread from which people will eat until the reckoning is finished." al-Abrash said: I said: "Indeed, the people on that day will be too preoccupied to eat." Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) said: "فهم في النار لا يشتغلون عن أكل الضريع وشرب الحميم ـ وهم في عذاب فكيف يشتغلون عنه في الحساب؟" — "They are in the Fire and are not preoccupied from eating the bitter thorn-fruit and drinking the boiling water—and they are in punishment—so how would they be preoccupied from it during the reckoning?"

I say: His saying: "transformed into a pure loaf of bread" may be a comparison, as might be understood from the following report.

In the Irshad of al-Mufid, and the Ihtijaj of al-Tabarsi, from Abd al-Rahman ibn Abd Allah al-Zuhri, he said: Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik performed the pilgrimage and entered the Sacred Mosque—leaning on the son of Salim his client—while Muhammad ibn Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام) was sitting in the mosque. Salim his client said to him: "O Commander of the Faithful, this is Muhammad ibn Ali." Hisham said: "The one by whom the people of Iraq are infatuated?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Go to him and say to him: The Commander of the Faithful says to you: What is it that people will eat and drink—until the judgment is made between them on the Day of Resurrection?" Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) said: "يحشر الناس على مثل قرص نقي فيها أنهار متفجرة ـ يأكلون ويشربون حتى يفرغ من الحساب ـ" — "People will be gathered upon something like a pure disk in which there are gushing rivers—they will eat and drink until the reckoning is finished."

He said: Hisham thought he had triumphed over him, so he said: "Allahu Akbar! Go to him and say: What will preoccupy them from eating and drinking on that day?" Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) said: "هم في النار أشغل ولم يشتغلوا عن ذلك ـ قالوا أفيضوا علينا من الماء أو مما رزقكم الله" — "Those in the Fire are more preoccupied, yet they were not preoccupied from that—they said: 'Pour upon us some water or some of what Allah has provided you.'" Hisham remained silent, not returning a word.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Aflah, the client of Abu Ayyub: that a man from the Jews asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ—what will it be transformed into?" He said: "خبزة" — "A loaf of bread." The Jew said: "درمكة بأبي أنت" — "A darmakah, may my father be sacrificed for you." He said: He laughed and then said: "قاتل الله اليهود هل تدرون ما الدرمكة؟ لباب الخبز" — "May Allah fight the Jews! Do you know what the darmakah is? It is the core of the bread."

In it, Ahmad, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu Nu'aym in al-Dala'il extracted from Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, who said: A rabbi from the Jews came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "Have you considered when Allah says: يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ—where will the creation be at that time?" He said: "أضياف الله لن يعجزهم ما لديه" — "The guests of Allah; what is with Him will not fail them."

I say: The variation of the narrations in interpreting the transformation is not without indication that they are parables struck for approximation. What is certain from the meaning of transformation is that the reality of the earth, the heaven, and what is in them on that day is what it is, except that the system prevailing in them on that day is other than the system prevailing in them in the world.

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of transmission from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: I heard Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) saying: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has created in the earth—since He created it—seven worlds who are not from the children of Adam. He created them from the surface of the earth—then He settled them in it one after another with their world.

Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, created Adam, the father of mankind—and created his progeny from him. No, by Allah, Paradise has not been empty of the souls of the believers since He created it, nor has the Fire been empty of the souls of the disbelievers and the sinners—since He, the Mighty and Majestic, created it. Perhaps you think that when it is the Day of Resurrection—and He makes the bodies of the people of Paradise with their souls in Paradise—and makes the bodies of the people of the Fire with their souls in the Fire—that Allah the Exalted will not be worshipped in His lands—nor will He create a creation who worship Him and profess His Oneness? Yes, by Allah, Allah will surely create a creation without males—nor females who will worship Him, profess His Oneness, and magnify Him—and He will create for them an earth to carry them and a heaven to shade them. Does not Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, say: يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَالسَّماواتُ? And the Mighty and Majestic has said: أَفَعَيِينا بِالْخَلْقِ الْأَوَّلِ بَلْ هُمْ فِي لَبْسٍ مِنْ خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ 'Were We exhausted by the first creation? Rather they are in doubt about a new creation.'" (50:15).

I say: al-Ayyashi narrated it in his Tafsir from Muhammad ibn Muslim from him (عليه‌السلام): similar to it, and it is other than the meanings we presented previously.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ, he said: "It will be transformed into a white loaf of bread in the standing place from which the believers will eat." Regarding وَتَرَى الْمُجْرِمِينَ يَوْمَئِذٍ مُقَرَّنِينَ فِي الْأَصْفادِ, he said: "He said: bound to one another." Regarding سَرابِيلُهُمْ مِنْ قَطِرانٍ, he said: "He said: the sarabīl are the shirts."

He said: In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام): regarding His saying: سَرابِيلُهُمْ مِنْ قَطِرانٍ, it is hot, molten copper whose heat has reached its limit—Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: وَتَغْشى وُجُوهَهُمُ النَّارُ—they are shirted in that copper and the Fire envelops their faces.

I say: He means that the intent of the two sentences: سَرابِيلُهُمْ مِنْ قَطِرانٍ وَتَغْشى وُجُوهَهُمُ النَّارُ together is an exposition that they are covered and enveloped; as for their bodies, it is with pitch, and as for their faces, it is with the Fire.
